If Entity T routinely provides 10% price concessions, the estimated transaction price is $200,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 10%) = $200,000 Г— 0.90 = $180,000, subject to the constraint on variable consideration (ASC 606-10-32-11).
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-32-7гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒеҖӨеј•гҒҚпјҲprice concessionsпјүгҒҜеӨүеӢ•еҜҫдҫЎгҒ®дёҖеҪўж…ӢгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮдјҒжҘӯгҒҜжЁ©еҲ©гӮ’еҫ—гӮӢгҒЁиҰӢиҫјгӮҖеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®йҮ‘йЎҚгӮ’иҰӢз©ҚгӮӮгӮӢеҝ…иҰҒгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮEntity TгҒҢйҖҡеёё10%гҒ®еҖӨеј•гҒҚгӮ’жҸҗдҫӣгҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒиҰӢз©ҚеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒҜ$200,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 10%) = $180,000гҒЁгҒӘгӮӢпјҲеӨүеӢ•еҜҫдҫЎгҒ®еҲ¶зҙ„(ASC 606-10-32-11)гҒ®и©•дҫЎгӮӮеҝ…иҰҒпјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Incremental Costs of Obtaining - Amortization Period
questions.stem: Entity U capitalizes a $60,000 sales commission as an incremental cost of obtaining a 2-year contract. The entity uses the practical expedient in ASC 340-40-25-4 because the amortization period would be one year or less. Under which condition is this practical expedient available?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The practical expedient is available only when the contract term is one year or less

choices.label: B
choices.text: The practical expedient is available when the amortization period of the asset that the entity would recognize is one year or less

choices.label: C
choices.text: The practical expedient is available whenever incremental costs exceed $50,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: The practical expedient is available only for commission costs, not other contract costs

questions.explanation: ASC 340-40-25-4 provides a practical expedient allowing an entity to expense incremental costs of obtaining a contract as incurred if the amortization period of the asset that the entity otherwise would have recognized is one year or less. The key factor is the amortization period, not the contract term вҖ” the amortization period may differ from the contract term when renewals or expected customer relationships are considered.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, which of the following is NOT an indicator of the transfer of control of a good at a point in time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity has a present right to payment for the asset

choices.label: B
choices.text: The customer has legal title to the asset

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity retains physical possession of the asset for its own use

choices.label: D
choices.text: The customer has accepted the asset

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-30 lists indicators of the transfer of control at a point in time: (a) the entity has a present right to payment, (b) the customer has legal title, (c) the entity has transferred physical possession, (d) the customer has the significant risks and rewards of ownership, and (e) the customer has accepted the asset. If the entity retains physical possession for its own use, that indicates control has NOT transferred to the customer.

questions.stem: Entity V provides janitorial services to a building owner on a monthly basis. The customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits of Entity V's cleaning services as they are performed. Under ASC 606, how should Entity V recognize revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the end of each month when the invoice is sent

choices.label: B
choices.text: Over time, as the services are performed

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the end of the contract term

choices.label: D
choices.text: When cash is collected from the building owner

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-27(a), a performance obligation is satisfied over time if the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the entity's performance as the entity performs. Janitorial services are a classic example вҖ” the building is cleaned as the service occurs, and the customer receives the benefit immediately. Revenue is recognized over time as the cleaning services are rendered.

questions.stem: Entity W contracts to build a customized factory for a customer. The contract includes design, engineering, procurement of materials, and construction. These activities are highly interrelated and Entity W provides a significant integration service. Under ASC 606, how many performance obligations does this contract contain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Four вҖ” one for each activity (design, engineering, procurement, construction)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two вҖ” design/engineering combined and procurement/construction combined

choices.label: C
choices.text: One вҖ” the combined output of a completed factory

choices.label: D
choices.text: Three вҖ” design, procurement, and combined engineering/construction

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-21(c), a good or service is not separately identifiable if it is highly interrelated or interdependent with other goods or services in the contract. When an entity provides a significant integration service that combines various inputs into a single combined output, the individual activities are not distinct. Because the design, engineering, procurement, and construction are highly interdependent and Entity W provides a significant integration service, there is one performance obligation вҖ” the completed factory.

questions.stem: A telecommunications company enters into a contract that includes activating the customer's service, which involves administrative setup in the company's systems. The activation does not transfer a good or service to the customer. Under ASC 606, how should the activation activity be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a separate performance obligation with revenue recognized at activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: As an administrative task that is not a performance obligation

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a combined performance obligation with the ongoing service

choices.label: D
choices.text: As a contract fulfillment cost that should be capitalized

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-17, activities that an entity must undertake to fulfill a contract but that do not transfer a good or service to the customer are not performance obligations. Activation is an administrative setup activity вҖ” it does not deliver a distinct good or service to the customer. Therefore, it is not a performance obligation. Any upfront fees charged for activation should be evaluated to determine if they relate to another performance obligation (such as the ongoing service).

questions.stem: Entity X sells Product A on a standalone basis for $50 to similar customers in similar circumstances. Entity X also sells Product A in a bundle with Product B. When allocating the transaction price for the bundle, what standalone selling price should Entity X use for Product A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $50, because the observable standalone selling price is the best evidence of SSP

choices.label: B
choices.text: An estimated price using the expected cost plus margin approach

choices.label: C
choices.text: An estimated price using the adjusted market assessment approach

choices.label: D
choices.text: The residual approach, since Product A is always sold in a bundle

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-32, the best evidence of a standalone selling price is the observable price when the entity sells that good or service separately in similar circumstances to similar customers. Since Entity X sells Product A standalone for $50, this observable price should be used. Estimation methods (expected cost plus margin, adjusted market assessment, residual approach) are only used when the SSP is not directly observable (ASC 606-10-32-33 through 32-35).

questions.stem: Entity Y has three separate contracts with Customer Z, entered into at different times over a two-year period. Each contract was negotiated independently, has its own pricing, and does not reference the other contracts. Under ASC 606-10-25-9, should these contracts be combined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because they are with the same customer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because ASC 606 requires all contracts with the same customer to be combined

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because none of the combination criteria in ASC 606-10-25-9 appear to be met

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because the contracts were entered at different times

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-9 requires combination of contracts only when specific criteria are met: (a) contracts are negotiated as a package with a single commercial objective, (b) consideration paid in one contract depends on the other, or (c) goods or services in the contracts are a single performance obligation. Simply being with the same customer is not sufficient. Because these contracts were negotiated independently at different times with independent pricing, none of the combination criteria appear to be met.

questions.stem: Entity AA has a $1,000,000 construction contract recognized over time using the cost-to-cost method. The contract requires milestone payments: $300,000 at 25% completion, $300,000 at 50% completion, and $400,000 at completion. At the end of Year 1, costs incurred are $400,000 of estimated total costs of $800,000. Entity AA has received the first milestone payment of $300,000. What is the contract asset (or liability) balance at the end of Year 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contract asset of $200,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contract asset of $100,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contract liability of $100,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contract asset of $500,000

questions.explanation: Percentage complete = $400,000 / $800,000 = 50%. Revenue recognized = $1,000,000 Г— 50% = $500,000. Cash received (milestone payments) = $300,000. Contract asset = Revenue recognized вҲ’ Cash received = $500,000 вҲ’ $300,000 = $200,000. The entity has recognized more revenue than it has billed/collected, resulting in a contract asset.
questions.explanationJa: йҖІжҚ—еәҰ = $400,000 / $800,000 = 50%гҖӮиӘҚиӯҳеҸҺзӣҠ = $1,000,000 Г— 50% = $500,000гҖӮеҸ—еҸ–зҸҫйҮ‘пјҲгғһгӮӨгғ«гӮ№гғҲгғјгғіж”Ҝжү•пјү = $300,000гҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„иіҮз”Ј = иӘҚиӯҳеҸҺзӣҠ вҲ’ еҸ—еҸ–зҸҫйҮ‘ = $500,000 вҲ’ $300,000 = $200,000гҖӮдјҒжҘӯгҒҜи«ӢжұӮгғ»еӣһеҸҺйЎҚгӮ’дёҠеӣһгӮӢеҸҺзӣҠгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒҰгҒҠгӮҠгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иіҮз”ЈгҒҢз”ҹгҒҳгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Progress Measures - Input vs Output Selection
questions.stem: Under ASC 606, which of the following factors should an entity consider when selecting a method to measure progress toward complete satisfaction of a performance obligation satisfied over time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The method must always be the cost-to-cost (input) method for construction contracts

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity should select the method that best depicts the transfer of control of goods or services to the customer

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity must use the output method whenever physical units are involved

choices.label: D
choices.text: The method should be chosen to maximize revenue recognition in earlier periods

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-55-17 states that in determining the appropriate method for measuring progress, an entity should consider the nature of the good or service that the entity promised to transfer. The objective is to select a method that faithfully depicts the entity's performance вҖ” that is, the transfer of control of goods or services to the customer. Both input methods and output methods may be appropriate, depending on the circumstances.

questions.stem: Entity BB has a $2,000,000 fixed-price construction contract. At the end of Year 2, the entity determines it can no longer reasonably measure progress toward completion. Costs incurred to date are $900,000, all of which are expected to be recoverable. Under ASC 606, what amount of revenue should Entity BB recognize cumulatively through Year 2?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-37, when an entity cannot reasonably measure the outcome of a performance obligation satisfied over time but expects to recover the costs incurred, the entity should recognize revenue only to the extent of costs incurred until such time as it can reasonably measure the outcome. This is sometimes called the zero-profit method. Total revenue recognized = $900,000 (equal to costs incurred).

questions.stem: A contractor enters into a $5,000,000 construction contract. The customer furnishes materials costing $800,000 directly to the job site. The contractor has no control over these customer-furnished materials. Under ASC 606, how should the contractor account for the customer-furnished materials when measuring progress using the cost-to-cost method?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include the $800,000 in both the numerator and denominator of the cost-to-cost calculation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exclude the $800,000 from the cost-to-cost calculation entirely

choices.label: C
choices.text: Include the $800,000 only in the denominator

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize $800,000 as separate revenue when materials are received

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-21, when using an input method such as cost-to-cost, the entity should exclude the effects of any inputs that do not depict the entity's performance in transferring control of goods or services to the customer. Customer-furnished materials are not costs incurred by the contractor and do not represent the contractor's effort or performance. Therefore, they should be excluded from the cost-to-cost calculation entirely.

questions.stem: Entity CC sells electronics with a 30-day return policy. Customers who return goods are charged a 15% restocking fee. Entity CC estimates 10% of sales will be returned. During the period, Entity CC made sales of $1,000,000. What refund liability should Entity CC recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-23, an entity that expects returns should recognize a refund liability for the amount of consideration expected to be refunded. Expected returns = $1,000,000 Г— 10% = $100,000. However, the 15% restocking fee reduces the refund obligation: Refund per return = $100,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 15%) = $100,000 Г— 85% = $85,000. The refund liability is $85,000 because the entity only refunds 85% of the returned goods' purchase price. The $15,000 restocking fee is retained by the entity.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-55-23гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒиҝ”е“ҒгҒҢиҰӢиҫјгҒҫгӮҢгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒиҝ”йҮ‘гҒҢиҰӢиҫјгҒҫгӮҢгӮӢеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®йҮ‘йЎҚгҒ«гҒӨгҒ„гҒҰиҝ”йҮ‘иІ еӮөгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮдәҲжғіиҝ”е“ҒйЎҚ = $1,000,000 Г— 10% = $100,000гҖӮгҒҹгҒ гҒ—гҖҒ15%гҒ®гғӘгӮ№гғҲгғғгӮӯгғігӮ°ж–ҷгҒҢиҝ”йҮ‘зҫ©еӢҷгӮ’жёӣе°‘гҒ•гҒӣгӮӢпјҡиҝ”йҮ‘йЎҚ = $100,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 15%) = $100,000 Г— 85% = $85,000гҖӮиҝ”йҮ‘иІ еӮөгҒҜ$85,000гҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒдјҒжҘӯгҒҜиҝ”е“ҒгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹе•Ҷе“ҒгҒ®иіје…ҘдҫЎж јгҒ®85%гҒ®гҒҝгӮ’иҝ”йҮ‘гҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ$15,000гҒ®гғӘгӮ№гғҲгғғгӮӯгғігӮ°ж–ҷгҒҜдјҒжҘӯгҒҢдҝқжҢҒгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: Entity DD receives a $240,000 advance payment on January 1 for a 2-year service contract. Services are provided evenly over the contract period. What journal entry should Entity DD record on January 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Debit Cash $240,000, Credit Revenue $240,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Debit Cash $240,000, Credit Contract Liability (Deferred Revenue) $240,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit Cash $240,000, Credit Contract Asset $240,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Debit Accounts Receivable $240,000, Credit Revenue $240,000

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-45-2, a contract liability is an entity's obligation to transfer goods or services to a customer for which the entity has received consideration. When Entity DD receives the $240,000 advance payment before performing services, it records Cash (debit) and Contract Liability / Deferred Revenue (credit). Revenue will be recognized as the services are performed вҖ” $10,000 per month ($240,000 / 24 months) over the 2-year period.

questions.stem: When adopting ASC 606 using the full retrospective method, which of the following is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity restates each prior reporting period presented in the financial statements as if ASC 606 had always been applied

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity records a cumulative catch-up adjustment to retained earnings at the beginning of the current period only

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity applies ASC 606 only to new contracts entered into after the adoption date

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prior period financial statements are not restated; only footnote disclosures are updated

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-65-1(c), the full retrospective method requires the entity to apply ASC 606 retrospectively to each prior reporting period presented in accordance with ASC 250 (Accounting Changes and Error Corrections). This means restating comparative financial statements as if ASC 606 had been applied since the inception of each contract. Recording a cumulative catch-up adjustment to retained earnings at the beginning of the current period only describes the modified retrospective method, not the full retrospective method.

questions.stem: Entity A has a $4,000,000 fixed-price contract recognized over time using the cost-to-cost input method. Estimated total costs at inception were $3,000,000. During Year 1, the entity incurred $1,200,000 of costs, of which $200,000 related to wasted materials and abnormal spoilage that were not anticipated in the original cost estimate and do not reflect progress. How much revenue should Entity A recognize in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-21, when using a cost-to-cost input method, costs that do not depict the entity's progress (such as wasted materials and abnormal spoilage) are excluded from the measure of progress. Costs reflecting progress = $1,200,000 - $200,000 = $1,000,000. Percentage complete = $1,000,000 / $3,000,000 = 33.33%. Revenue = $4,000,000 x 33.33% = $1,333,333.

questions.stem: Entity B has an $8,000,000 construction contract recognized over time using the cost-to-cost input method. Estimated total costs are $6,000,000, which include $1,200,000 for a specialized generator. The generator is purchased and delivered to the site at year-end (the customer controls it on delivery), but it will be installed late in the project, and Entity B is not the principal providing a significant integration service for the generator itself. At year-end, total costs incurred are $2,400,000, including the $1,200,000 generator. Applying the uninstalled-materials guidance, how much revenue should Entity B recognize for the year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-21(c), for uninstalled materials that the customer controls but for which the entity is not the principal providing significant integration/customization, the entity recognizes revenue on those goods only to the extent of their cost (zero margin) and excludes them from the cost-to-cost measure of progress. Step 1 - Progress excluding the generator: costs incurred excluding generator = $2,400,000 - $1,200,000 = $1,200,000; total estimated costs excluding generator = $6,000,000 - $1,200,000 = $4,800,000; percentage = $1,200,000 / $4,800,000 = 25%. Step 2 - Revenue on the non-generator work = ($8,000,000 - $1,200,000) x 25% = $6,800,000 x 25% = $1,700,000. Step 3 - Add the generator at cost (zero margin) = $1,200,000. Total revenue = $1,700,000 + $1,200,000 = $2,900,000.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-55-21(c)гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒҢж”Ҝй…ҚгҒҷгӮӢжҚ®д»ҳеүҚиіҮжқҗгҒ§гҖҒдјҒжҘӯгҒҢйҮҚиҰҒгҒӘзөұеҗҲ/гӮ«гӮ№гӮҝгғһгӮӨгӮәгҒ®дё»дҪ“гҒ§гҒӘгҒ„гӮӮгҒ®гҒҜгҖҒгҒқгҒ®гӮігӮ№гғҲгҒ®зҜ„еӣІпјҲгғһгғјгӮёгғігӮјгғӯпјүгҒ§гҒ®гҒҝеҸҺзӣҠгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҖҒгӮігӮ№гғҲеҜҫгӮігӮ№гғҲйҖІжҚ—еәҰгҒӢгӮүйҷӨеӨ–гҒҷгӮӢгҖӮгӮ№гғҶгғғгғ—1 - зҷәйӣ»ж©ҹгӮ’йҷӨгҒҸйҖІжҚ—еәҰпјҡзҷәйӣ»ж©ҹгӮ’йҷӨгҒҸзҷәз”ҹгӮігӮ№гғҲ = $2,400,000 - $1,200,000 = $1,200,000гҖҒзҷәйӣ»ж©ҹгӮ’йҷӨгҒҸиҰӢз©Қз·ҸгӮігӮ№гғҲ = $6,000,000 - $1,200,000 = $4,800,000гҖҒеүІеҗҲ = 25%гҖӮгӮ№гғҶгғғгғ—2 - зҷәйӣ»ж©ҹд»ҘеӨ–гҒ®еҸҺзӣҠ = ($8,000,000 - $1,200,000) Г— 25% = $6,800,000 Г— 25% = $1,700,000гҖӮгӮ№гғҶгғғгғ—3 - зҷәйӣ»ж©ҹгӮ’гӮігӮ№гғҲгҒ§еҠ з®— = $1,200,000гҖӮеҸҺзӣҠеҗҲиЁҲ = $1,700,000 + $1,200,000 = $2,900,000гҖӮ

questions.stem: Entity C sells a product for $40 per unit. The contract provides a retrospective volume rebate: if the customer purchases more than 1,000 units in the calendar year, the price for ALL units purchased drops to $36 per unit. In Q1, the customer buys 300 units. Entity C estimates, using the expected value method, an 80% probability the customer will exceed 1,000 units. How much revenue should Entity C recognize in Q1?
questions.explanation: A retrospective volume rebate is variable consideration affecting the price of all units. Under ASC 606-10-32-8, using the expected value method with a high probability (80%) of exceeding the threshold, the entity estimates the price per unit it expects to be entitled to. Because the rebate is highly likely, the entity recognizes Q1 sales at the discounted price: 300 units x $36 = $10,800. The $4 per unit difference (vs. the $40 invoiced) is recorded as a refund liability of $1,200.

questions.subtopic: Significant Financing - Advance Payment by Customer
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Entity D receives a $100,000 advance payment from a customer for a single piece of equipment that will be delivered on December 31, Year 2 (two years later). The contract contains a significant financing component with an imputed interest rate of 6%. What amount of revenue should Entity D recognize upon delivery of the equipment on December 31, Year 2?
questions.explanation: When a customer pays in advance, the entity effectively receives financing from the customer, so the entity recognizes interest EXPENSE and increases the contract liability over time; revenue at delivery equals the cash received plus accrued interest. Year 1 interest = $100,000 x 6% = $6,000; contract liability end of Year 1 = $106,000. Year 2 interest = $106,000 x 6% = $6,360; contract liability end of Year 2 = $112,360. Revenue recognized at delivery = $112,360. (Equivalent to $100,000 x 1.06^2 = $112,360.)
questions.explanationJa: йЎ§е®ўгҒҢеүҚжү•гҒ„гҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒдјҒжҘӯгҒҜе®ҹиіӘзҡ„гҒ«йЎ§е®ўгҒӢгӮүиіҮйҮ‘гӮ’еҸ—гҒ‘еҸ–гӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒеҲ©жҒҜиІ»з”ЁгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒ—еҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгӮ’еў—еҠ гҒ•гҒӣгӮӢгҖӮеј•жёЎжҷӮгҒ®еҸҺзӣҠгҒҜеҸ—й ҳзҸҫйҮ‘гҒ«зҷәз”ҹеҲ©жҒҜгӮ’еҠ гҒҲгҒҹйЎҚгҒЁгҒӘгӮӢгҖӮз¬¬1е№ҙеҲ©жҒҜ = $100,000 Г— 6% = $6,000гҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮө = $106,000гҖӮз¬¬2е№ҙеҲ©жҒҜ = $106,000 Г— 6% = $6,360гҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮө = $112,360гҖӮеј•жёЎжҷӮеҸҺзӣҠ = $112,360пјҲ= $100,000 Г— 1.06^2пјүгҖӮ

questions.stem: Entity E sells 2,000 units at $60 each. The cost of each unit is $35. Entity E grants a right of return and estimates 6% of units will be returned. Returned units are expected to be resold with no decline in value, but Entity E estimates $2 per returned unit in restoration/handling costs. What is the carrying amount of the asset for the right to recover products that Entity E should recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-26, the asset for the right to recover products is measured at the former carrying amount of the inventory, less any expected costs to recover the products (including potential decreases in value). Units expected to be returned = 2,000 x 6% = 120. Former carrying amount = 120 x $35 = $4,200. Less restoration costs = 120 x $2 = $240. Right-to-recover asset = $4,200 - $240 = $3,960.

questions.subtopic: Allocating Variable Consideration to One Obligation
questions.stem: Entity F enters a contract with two distinct performance obligations: Product G (SSP $500,000) and a maintenance service (SSP $100,000). The contract price is $600,000 plus a $50,000 bonus contingent solely on the timely completion of the maintenance service. The variable bonus relates entirely to the maintenance obligation, and allocating it entirely to maintenance meets the allocation objective. How much total transaction price should be allocated to the maintenance service?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-40, variable consideration may be allocated entirely to one performance obligation if (a) the variable payment relates specifically to satisfying that obligation, and (b) allocating it entirely to that obligation is consistent with the overall allocation objective. The $50,000 bonus relates solely to the maintenance service. Fixed portion: maintenance gets $600,000 x ($100,000/$600,000) = $100,000. Plus the entire $50,000 bonus = $100,000 + $50,000 = $150,000 allocated to maintenance.

questions.stem: Entity H, a manufacturer, enters a contract to produce custom parts. The customer agrees to pay $80,000 cash and also to supply raw materials (fair value $30,000) that Entity H will use in production. Entity H obtains control of the contributed materials. Under ASC 606, what is the total transaction price for this contract?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-21 and 32-24, if a customer contributes goods or services (e.g., materials) to facilitate fulfillment, and the entity obtains control of those contributed goods, the entity accounts for them as noncash consideration measured at fair value. Transaction price = cash $80,000 + fair value of contributed materials $30,000 = $110,000.

questions.subtopic: Principal vs Agent - Gross Revenue When Principal
questions.stem: Entity J operates an online marketplace. For a particular product line, Entity J purchases inventory, takes title and physical control before any sale, sets the prices, and bears inventory and credit risk. It sells the goods to end customers for $1,000 and the original supplier was paid $700. Entity J concludes it is the principal. How much revenue and what related cost should Entity J recognize?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Revenue of $1,000 and cost of goods sold of $700

choices.label: B
choices.text: Revenue of $300 (net commission)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Revenue of $700 and cost of goods sold of $700

choices.label: D
choices.text: Revenue of $1,000 and no cost of goods sold

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-37, an entity is a principal if it controls the specified good or service before it is transferred to the customer. Indicators that Entity J controls the goods include taking title/physical possession, setting prices, and bearing inventory and credit risk. As principal, Entity J recognizes revenue on a gross basis ($1,000) and records the $700 paid to the supplier as cost of goods sold.

questions.subtopic: Onerous Estimate - Constraint on Refund Reversal
questions.stem: Entity K sells equipment for $90,000 that includes a 60-day right of return. This is a new product, the customer base is unfamiliar, and Entity K has no historical return data for comparable products, making the level of returns highly uncertain. The equipment cost $55,000. Under ASC 606, how much revenue should Entity K recognize at the point of sale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $0 until the return right lapses or returns can be reliably estimated

choices.label: B
choices.text: $90,000 immediately

choices.label: C
choices.text: $85,500 (assuming 5% returns)

choices.label: D
choices.text: $35,000 (gross profit only)

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-11 and 55-23, revenue is recognized only to the extent it is probable a significant reversal will not occur. When an entity cannot reasonably estimate returns (no history, new product, highly uncertain), it cannot conclude that a significant reversal is improbable for any portion. Therefore, no revenue is recognized until the uncertainty is resolved (returns become estimable or the return period lapses). The amounts received are recorded as a refund liability and the product as a right-to-recover asset.

questions.subtopic: Material Right - Allocation to Discounted Option
questions.stem: Entity L sells a machine for $20,000 and grants the customer an option to buy a second identical machine within 90 days for $15,000 (a $5,000 discount off the normal $20,000 price). Entity L estimates a 60% likelihood the option will be exercised. The standalone selling price of the option, reflecting the discount and exercise likelihood, is $3,000. How much of the $20,000 should be allocated to the machine delivered now?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-42, an option that provides a material right is a separate performance obligation. Allocate the $20,000 based on relative SSPs. SSP of machine = $20,000; SSP of option = $3,000; total SSP = $23,000. Machine allocation = $20,000 x ($20,000 / $23,000) = $20,000 x 0.86957 = $17,391. The remaining $2,609 is deferred as a contract liability until the option is exercised or expires.

questions.subtopic: Contract Liability Recognized at Period End
questions.stem: On October 1, Year 1, Entity M signs a one-year contract to provide a customer with monthly software analytics for a total fixed fee of $36,000, billed and collected in advance. Services are delivered evenly each month. What amount should Entity M report as revenue for the year ended December 31, Year 1, and as a contract liability at December 31, Year 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Revenue $9,000; contract liability $27,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Revenue $36,000; contract liability $0

choices.label: C
choices.text: Revenue $12,000; contract liability $24,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Revenue $27,000; contract liability $9,000

questions.explanation: Services are provided evenly over 12 months. Monthly revenue = $36,000 / 12 = $3,000. From October 1 to December 31 = 3 months delivered. Revenue Year 1 = 3 x $3,000 = $9,000. The remaining cash received but not yet earned is a contract liability = $36,000 - $9,000 = $27,000 (representing the obligation to provide 9 more months of service).

questions.stem: Entity N sells specialized equipment to a customer and meets all four bill-and-hold criteria, so control transfers and revenue is recognized for the equipment. Under the same arrangement, Entity N also agrees to provide custodial storage and insurance for the equipment for the next six months until the customer takes physical possession. Under ASC 606, how should the custodial service be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a separate performance obligation to which a portion of the transaction price is allocated

choices.label: B
choices.text: As part of the equipment sale, with all revenue recognized when control of the equipment transfers

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a reduction of the equipment revenue

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is ignored because it is incidental to the sale

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-84, in a bill-and-hold arrangement an entity must consider whether it has any remaining performance obligations (e.g., custodial services) to which it must allocate a portion of the transaction price. The custodial storage/insurance service is a distinct service provided over the six-month period and is therefore a separate performance obligation. A portion of the transaction price is allocated to it and recognized over time as the custodial service is provided.

questions.stem: Entity P licenses a proprietary drug formula to a customer and also agrees to manufacture the drug for the customer. The customer cannot benefit from the license without Entity P's manufacturing because the manufacturing process is proprietary and unique to Entity P, and no other party can produce the drug. Under ASC 606, how should the license and manufacturing be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a single combined performance obligation because the license is not distinct from the manufacturing

choices.label: B
choices.text: As two separate performance obligations, recognizing the license at a point in time

choices.label: C
choices.text: The license is recognized over time and the manufacturing at a point in time

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the manufacturing is a performance obligation; the license is ignored

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-56, a license is not distinct from other promised goods or services when the customer cannot derive benefit from the license without those other goods or services. Because the customer cannot benefit from the drug formula license without Entity P's unique, proprietary manufacturing (no other party can produce the drug), the license is not distinct and is combined with the manufacturing into a single performance obligation. Revenue is recognized based on the combined obligation's transfer pattern.

questions.stem: Entity Q sells a subscription with tiered usage pricing. The customer is charged $2 per transaction for the first 10,000 transactions in a quarter and $1.50 per transaction for transactions above 10,000. Entity Q estimates the customer will process 14,000 transactions in Q1. Assuming the tiers reflect the standalone value of each increment of service (no material right), what revenue should Entity Q expect to recognize for Q1?
questions.explanation: When tiered pricing reflects the standalone value of each increment of distinct service (a series satisfied over time, with usage-based fees allocated to the period of usage under ASC 606-10-32-40), revenue follows the actual transaction tiers. First 10,000 transactions x $2 = $20,000. Next 4,000 transactions x $1.50 = $6,000. Total Q1 revenue = $20,000 + $6,000 = $26,000.

questions.subtopic: Contract Liability and Receivable - Unconditional Right
questions.stem: On December 1, Year 1, Entity R enters a noncancellable contract to deliver goods on February 1, Year 2. The contract requires the customer to pay the $50,000 price on January 1, Year 2 (before delivery). Entity R has an unconditional right to the consideration on January 1. What entry, if any, should Entity R record on January 1, Year 2, when the payment becomes due (assume not yet paid)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Debit Receivable $50,000; Credit Contract Liability $50,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Debit Receivable $50,000; Credit Revenue $50,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit Contract Asset $50,000; Credit Revenue $50,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: No entry until the goods are delivered on February 1

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-45-4, an entity presents a receivable when it has an unconditional right to consideration (conditioned only on the passage of time). Because the contract is noncancellable and payment is due January 1 regardless of delivery, Entity R has an unconditional right to $50,000 on January 1 even though it has not yet performed. It recognizes a receivable and a corresponding contract liability (deferred revenue), since the performance obligation is unsatisfied. Revenue is recognized later upon delivery on February 1.

questions.stem: Entity S signs a 3-year contract to provide monthly payroll processing. Each month of service is distinct and substantially the same with the same pattern of transfer, so it is accounted for as a single performance obligation (a series) satisfied over time. The total fixed price is $180,000. Under the series guidance, which measure of progress is appropriate, and how much revenue is recognized per month?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single measure of progress applies to the entire series; revenue of $5,000 per month (time-based)

choices.label: B
choices.text: A separate measure of progress for each month; revenue varies by month's effort

choices.label: C
choices.text: Revenue is recognized only at the end of each annual period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Revenue of $60,000 is recognized at the start of each year

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-14(b) and 25-15, when a series of distinct goods or services is accounted for as a single performance obligation, a single method of measuring progress must be applied to the entire performance obligation. For payroll processing delivered evenly over 36 months, a time-based measure is appropriate. Revenue per month = $180,000 / 36 = $5,000.

questions.stem: Under the five-step revenue model of ASC 606, which step involves determining the standalone selling prices of each performance obligation and apportioning the contract consideration among them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Step 4 - Allocate the transaction price to the performance obligations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Step 2 - Identify the performance obligations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Step 3 - Determine the transaction price

choices.label: D
choices.text: Step 5 - Recognize revenue

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-28 through 32-41, Step 4 (Allocate the transaction price) requires the entity to allocate the transaction price to each performance obligation generally in proportion to relative standalone selling prices. Determining SSPs and apportioning the consideration are core activities of Step 4. Step 3 only determines the total transaction price; Step 4 distributes it.

questions.stem: Entity T sells a fleet of vehicles to a customer for $1,000,000 and guarantees the customer that the vehicles will have a resale value of at least $300,000 after three years; if not, Entity T will pay the shortfall. Entity T does not have an obligation or right to repurchase the vehicles. Control of the vehicles transfers to the customer at delivery. How should Entity T treat the residual value guarantee?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize revenue for the sale and account for the guarantee as a separate obligation under ASC 460 (guarantees)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Account for the entire transaction as a financing arrangement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defer all revenue until the three-year guarantee period expires

choices.label: D
choices.text: Account for the transaction as an operating lease

questions.explanation: Because Entity T has no right or obligation to repurchase the vehicles, the repurchase-agreement guidance (ASC 606-10-55-66 to 55-78) does not apply, and control transfers at delivery. The sale is recognized as revenue. The separate promise to compensate the customer if resale value falls below $300,000 is a guarantee accounted for under ASC 460, not a reduction of the transaction price or a repurchase obligation. A liability is recognized for the fair value of the guarantee at inception.

questions.subtopic: Performance Obligation Satisfied at a Point in Time - Risks and Rewards
questions.stem: Entity U sells goods FOB shipping point. Title and the significant risks and rewards of ownership pass to the customer when the goods are handed to the carrier on March 30, Year 1. The goods arrive at the customer on April 3, Year 1. Entity U has a present right to payment and the customer has accepted the terms. In which period should Entity U recognize revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: March, Year 1, when the goods are handed to the carrier (control transfers at shipping point)

choices.label: B
choices.text: April, Year 1, when the goods physically arrive at the customer

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the customer pays for the goods

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ratably between March 30 and April 3

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-30, control transfers at a point in time based on indicators including legal title, physical possession, risks and rewards, present right to payment, and customer acceptance. For FOB shipping point terms, title and the risks and rewards transfer when the goods are delivered to the carrier. Because Entity U also has a present right to payment, control transfers on March 30, and revenue is recognized in March, Year 1.

questions.stem: Entity V sells goods to a retailer for $500,000 and separately agrees to pay the retailer a $20,000 cooperative advertising allowance. Entity V receives a distinct advertising service from the retailer with a fair value of $12,000; the remaining $8,000 exceeds the fair value of the service received. Under ASC 606, how much revenue should Entity V recognize on the sale of goods?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-26, consideration payable to a customer is a reduction of the transaction price unless it is a payment for a distinct good or service received, in which case it is accounted for as a purchase (expense) up to the fair value of that good or service. Any excess over fair value reduces revenue. The $12,000 fair-value portion is an advertising expense; the excess $8,000 ($20,000 - $12,000) reduces revenue. Revenue = $500,000 - $8,000 = $492,000.

questions.subtopic: Combined Transaction Price - Multiple Performance Obligations
questions.stem: Entity W enters a contract with three distinct performance obligations: Hardware (SSP $120,000), Software (SSP $80,000), and a 1-year support service (SSP $50,000). The total contract price is $200,000. The discount does not relate to any specific obligation and is allocated proportionately. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the support service?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-31, the transaction price is allocated based on relative standalone selling prices. Total SSP = $120,000 + $80,000 + $50,000 = $250,000. Support allocation = $200,000 x ($50,000 / $250,000) = $200,000 x 0.20 = $40,000. (Hardware = $96,000; Software = $64,000; total = $200,000.)

questions.subtopic: Recognition When Right to Payment Exists (Over Time Criterion c)
questions.stem: Entity X manufactures a highly customized asset that has no alternative use to Entity X because it is configured to one customer's unique specifications and cannot be redirected. The contract gives Entity X an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date, including a reasonable profit margin, if the customer cancels. Under ASC 606, how should Entity X recognize revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Over time, because the asset has no alternative use and there is an enforceable right to payment for work completed to date

choices.label: B
choices.text: At a point in time when the asset is completed and delivered

choices.label: C
choices.text: At a point in time when the customer pays

choices.label: D
choices.text: Over time only if the customer controls the asset as it is built

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-27(c), a performance obligation is satisfied over time if the entity's performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the entity AND the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. Both conditions are met: the customized asset has no alternative use, and Entity X has an enforceable right to payment (cost plus reasonable margin) upon cancellation. Therefore, revenue is recognized over time.

questions.subtopic: Contract Costs - Impairment of Capitalized Asset
questions.stem: Entity Y has a capitalized contract asset of $50,000 representing costs to obtain a contract. The remaining consideration the entity expects to receive for the goods/services to which the asset relates is $42,000, and the remaining costs that relate directly to providing those goods/services are $5,000. Under ASC 340-40, what impairment loss, if any, should Entity Y recognize on the capitalized asset?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 340-40-35-3, an impairment loss is recognized to the extent the carrying amount of the asset exceeds (a) the remaining consideration the entity expects to receive for the related goods/services, LESS (b) the remaining costs that relate directly to providing those goods/services. Recoverable amount = $42,000 - $5,000 = $37,000. Carrying amount = $50,000. Impairment loss = $50,000 - $37,000 = $13,000.

questions.stem: Entity Z incurs $90,000 of costs to set up resources to fulfill a long-term service contract. These costs relate directly to the contract, generate resources that will be used to satisfy the performance obligation in the future, and are expected to be recovered. The costs are not within the scope of other guidance (e.g., inventory or PP&E). Under ASC 340-40, how should Entity Z account for these fulfillment costs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize as an asset and amortize consistent with the transfer of the related goods or services

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expense immediately as incurred

choices.label: C
choices.text: Record as a reduction of the transaction price

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize but do not amortize until the contract ends

questions.explanation: Under ASC 340-40-25-5, costs to fulfill a contract (not within the scope of other guidance) are capitalized only if all three criteria are met: (a) the costs relate directly to a contract, (b) the costs generate or enhance resources that will be used to satisfy performance obligations in the future, and (c) the costs are expected to be recovered. All three are met here. Per ASC 340-40-35-1, the capitalized asset is amortized on a systematic basis consistent with the transfer of the related goods or services.

questions.subtopic: Cumulative Catch-Up from Change in Estimate
questions.stem: Entity AA has a $3,000,000 contract recognized over time using cost-to-cost. At the end of Year 1, costs incurred were $600,000 and estimated total costs were $2,000,000, so revenue of $900,000 was recognized. During Year 2, the entity revises its estimated total costs to $2,400,000. By the end of Year 2, cumulative costs incurred are $1,440,000. How much revenue should Entity AA recognize in Year 2?
questions.explanation: A change in the estimated total cost is a change in accounting estimate, accounted for prospectively via cumulative catch-up. Cumulative percentage at end of Year 2 = $1,440,000 / $2,400,000 = 60%. Cumulative revenue = $3,000,000 x 60% = $1,800,000. Year 2 revenue = cumulative revenue - prior recognition = $1,800,000 - $900,000 = $900,000.

questions.stem: Entity BB sells 5,000 appliances for $400 each during the year. Each appliance carries a standard one-year assurance-type warranty (not separately priced). Entity BB estimates warranty repair costs of $12 per unit. Under ASC 606 and ASC 460, what should Entity BB record for the warranty at the time of sale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A warranty liability and expense of $60,000; recognize the full $2,000,000 as revenue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defer $60,000 of revenue as a separate performance obligation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allocate a portion of the $2,000,000 to the warranty obligation

choices.label: D
choices.text: No accrual; recognize warranty costs only when incurred

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-19 and ASC 460, an assurance-type warranty (one that assures the product meets agreed specifications and is not separately priced) is NOT a separate performance obligation. The full transaction price is recognized as revenue: 5,000 x $400 = $2,000,000. The estimated warranty cost is accrued as a liability and expense at the time of sale: 5,000 x $12 = $60,000.

questions.subtopic: Step 3 - Most Likely Amount with Penalty
questions.stem: Entity CC contracts to complete a project for $500,000, but the contract imposes a $50,000 penalty (reducing the price to $450,000) if the project is not completed by a specified deadline. The two outcomes are: on-time ($500,000) or late ($450,000). Entity CC believes there is a 90% probability of on-time completion. Using the most likely amount method, what transaction price should Entity CC use?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-8, the most likely amount method selects the single most likely outcome from a contract with two possible outcomes. Because on-time completion has a 90% probability (the single most likely outcome), the transaction price is $500,000 (no penalty). The expected-value figure would be $495,000 (0.90 x $500,000 + 0.10 x $450,000), but the most likely amount method uses the single most likely result, subject to the constraint.

questions.stem: Entity DD agreed to sell 200 widgets at $100 each ($20,000 total). After delivering 80 widgets, the parties modify the contract to add 100 more widgets. The additional 100 widgets are distinct, but the modification price is $90 each, which is below their $100 standalone selling price at the modification date. The modification therefore does not qualify as a separate contract and is treated as a termination of the old contract and creation of a new one. What is the price per widget that Entity DD should apply to ALL remaining undelivered widgets after the modification?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-13(a), when remaining goods are distinct but the modification is not a separate contract, the entity treats it as a termination and creation of a new contract. The new price is a blend of (1) consideration not yet recognized from the original contract and (2) consideration in the modification, spread over remaining units. Remaining original units = 200 - 80 = 120 at $100 = $12,000. Additional units = 100 at $90 = $9,000. Total remaining consideration = $12,000 + $9,000 = $21,000. Total remaining units = 120 + 100 = 220. Blended price = $21,000 / 220 = $95.45 per widget.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-25-13(a)гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒж®ӢдҪҷгҒ®иІЎгҒҢеҢәеҲҘеҸҜиғҪгҒ гҒҢеҲҘеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ«и©ІеҪ“гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгҖҒж—§еҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®зөӮдәҶгҒЁж–°еҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®еүөеҮәгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮж–°дҫЎж јгҒҜгҖҒ(1)ж—§еҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®жңӘиӘҚиӯҳеҜҫдҫЎгҒЁ(2)еӨүжӣҙеҲҶгҒ®еҜҫдҫЎгӮ’ж®ӢдҪҷеҚҳдҪҚгҒ«жҢүеҲҶгҒ—гҒҹгғ–гғ¬гғігғүдҫЎж јгҒЁгҒӘгӮӢгҖӮж—§еҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®ж®ӢгӮҠ = 200 - 80 = 120еҖӢ Г— $100 = $12,000гҖӮиҝҪеҠ  = 100еҖӢ Г— $90 = $9,000гҖӮж®ӢдҪҷеҜҫдҫЎеҗҲиЁҲ = $21,000гҖӮж®ӢдҪҷеҚҳдҪҚеҗҲиЁҲ = 220еҖӢгҖӮгғ–гғ¬гғігғүдҫЎж ј = $21,000 / 220 = $95.45/еҖӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, which of the following must an entity disclose regarding the significant judgments made in applying the revenue standard?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The methods, inputs, and assumptions used to determine the transaction price and to allocate it, and the timing of satisfaction of performance obligations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the total amount of revenue recognized during the period

choices.label: C
choices.text: The names of all customers representing more than 5% of revenue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Significant judgment disclosures are required only for public companies with over $1 billion in revenue

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-50-17 through 50-20, an entity must disclose the significant judgments, and changes in judgments, made in applying ASC 606. This includes the methods, inputs, and assumptions used to determine the transaction price (e.g., estimating variable consideration, assessing the constraint, adjusting for financing components), allocating the transaction price, and measuring obligations for returns and refunds, as well as the timing of satisfaction of performance obligations.

questions.stem: Entity EE sells a machine to a customer for $200,000. Entity EE retains a call option to repurchase the machine in 18 months for $130,000. At the time of sale, the expected market value of the machine in 18 months is $150,000, so the repurchase price is below the expected market value (and below the original selling price). Under ASC 606, how should Entity EE account for this arrangement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a lease, because the customer does not obtain control and the repurchase price is less than the original selling price

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a sale with a right of return

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a financing arrangement

choices.label: D
choices.text: As an outright sale with revenue of $200,000

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-68, when an entity has a call option to repurchase the asset, the customer does not obtain control because the customer is limited in its ability to use the asset. If the repurchase price is LESS than the original selling price, the contract is accounted for as a lease under ASC 842 (unless part of a sale-leaseback). Because the $130,000 call price is below the $200,000 original selling price, Entity EE accounts for the arrangement as a lease, not a sale.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-55-68гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒдјҒжҘӯгҒҢиіҮз”ЈиІ·жҲ»гҒ—гҒ®гӮігғјгғ«гӮӘгғ—гӮ·гғ§гғігӮ’жңүгҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒҜиіҮз”ЈгҒ®дҪҝз”ЁгҒҢеҲ¶йҷҗгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒж”Ҝй…ҚгӮ’зҚІеҫ—гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮиІ·жҲ»дҫЎж јгҒҢеҪ“еҲқиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгӮ’дёӢеӣһгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒASC 842гҒ«еҹәгҒҘгҒҸгғӘгғјгӮ№гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢпјҲгӮ»гғјгғ«гғ»гӮўгғігғүгғ»гғӘгғјгӮ№гғҗгғғгӮҜгҒ®дёҖйғЁгҒ§гҒӘгҒ„йҷҗгӮҠпјүгҖӮ$130,000гҒ®гӮігғјгғ«дҫЎж јгҒҢ$200,000гҒ®еҪ“еҲқиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгӮ’дёӢеӣһгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒEntity EEгҒҜиІ©еЈІгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгғӘгғјгӮ№гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Step 5 - Measure of Progress Cannot Be Determined Early
questions.stem: Entity FF begins a performance obligation satisfied over time in the first month of a contract. In that first month, Entity FF cannot yet reasonably measure its progress toward complete satisfaction, but it expects to recover the costs incurred. Costs incurred in month 1 are $40,000, all recoverable. The contract price is $500,000 with expected total costs of $300,000. How much revenue should Entity FF recognize in month 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-37, if an entity cannot reasonably measure its progress toward complete satisfaction of a performance obligation but expects to recover the costs incurred, it recognizes revenue only to the extent of the costs incurred until it can reasonably measure progress (the zero-profit/cost-recovery approach). Revenue in month 1 = costs incurred = $40,000 (with zero profit).

questions.stem: Which of the following factors would indicate that a contract does NOT contain a significant financing component, even though there is a difference between the promised consideration and the cash selling price?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The customer paid in advance and the timing of transfer is at the customer's discretion

choices.label: B
choices.text: The interest rate implied in the contract is substantially above the market rate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Payment is due five years after delivery with a stated zero interest rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: The difference arises solely from the time value of money over a three-year payment period

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-17, a contract does not have a significant financing component if the timing of transfer of goods or services is at the discretion of the customer (such as a prepaid customer loyalty/stored-value arrangement where the customer chooses when to take the goods). In that case, the difference between consideration and cash price is not attributable to financing. The other three situations all describe cases where a financing component is more likely to exist.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-32-17гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒиІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒ®з§»и»ўжҷӮжңҹгҒҢйЎ§е®ўгҒ®иЈҒйҮҸгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢе ҙеҗҲпјҲеүҚжү•гҒ„гҒ®гғӯгӮӨгғӨгғ«гғҶгӮЈ/гӮ№гғҲгӮўгғүгғҗгғӘгғҘгғјеҸ–жұәгӮҒгҒ§йЎ§е®ўгҒҢеҸ—й ҳжҷӮжңҹгӮ’йҒёгҒ¶е ҙеҗҲгҒӘгҒ©пјүгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ«йҮҚиҰҒгҒӘйҮ‘иһҚиҰҒзҙ гҒҜеӯҳеңЁгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮгҒқгҒ®е ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҜҫдҫЎгҒЁзҸҫйҮ‘дҫЎж јгҒ®е·®йЎҚгҒҜйҮ‘иһҚгҒ«иө·еӣ гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮд»–гҒ®3гҒӨгҒ®зҠ¶жіҒгҒҜгҒ„гҒҡгӮҢгӮӮйҮ‘иһҚиҰҒзҙ гҒҢеӯҳеңЁгҒҷгӮӢеҸҜиғҪжҖ§гҒҢй«ҳгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Sale with Customer Option - Material Right Test
questions.stem: Entity GG sells a printer for $300. As part of the sale, the customer receives a coupon for 25% off any ink cartridges purchased in the next 12 months. Other customers who did not buy a printer can also obtain the same 25%-off ink coupon through a public promotion available to anyone. Does the coupon represent a material right that is a separate performance obligation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the discount is available to all customers regardless of the printer purchase, so it does not provide a right the customer would not otherwise receive

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, any discount coupon is always a material right

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the coupon has economic value to the customer

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because coupons are never performance obligations under ASC 606

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-42 and 55-43, an option gives rise to a material right only if it provides the customer a right it would not receive without entering into the contract (e.g., a discount that exceeds the range of discounts typically given to that class of customer). Because the same 25%-off ink coupon is available to anyone through a public promotion, the customer who bought the printer is not receiving an incremental right. Therefore, the coupon does not provide a material right and is not a separate performance obligation.

questions.subtopic: Allocation of Transaction Price Changes After Inception
questions.stem: Entity HH has a contract with two performance obligations originally allocated $300,000 to Product A (delivered and recognized) and $200,000 to Service B (not yet performed), based on relative SSPs. After Product A is delivered, the transaction price increases by $60,000 due to a change in variable consideration that relates to the contract as a whole. Under ASC 606, how should the $60,000 increase be allocated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $36,000 to Product A (recognized immediately as revenue) and $24,000 to Service B

choices.label: B
choices.text: $60,000 entirely to Service B because Product A is already delivered

choices.label: C
choices.text: $60,000 entirely to Product A as a catch-up

choices.label: D
choices.text: $30,000 to each obligation equally

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-43, a change in the transaction price is allocated to the performance obligations on the same basis as at contract inception (relative SSPs), unless the change relates to a specific obligation. Original SSP basis: Product A $300,000 / total $500,000 = 60%; Service B = 40%. Allocation of the $60,000 increase: Product A = $60,000 x 60% = $36,000; Service B = $60,000 x 40% = $24,000. The $36,000 allocated to the already-satisfied Product A is recognized as revenue immediately (cumulative catch-up); the $24,000 for Service B is recognized as that service is performed.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-32-43гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒ®еӨүеӢ•гҒҜгҖҒзү№е®ҡгҒ®зҫ©еӢҷгҒ«й–ўйҖЈгҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгӮ’йҷӨгҒҚгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„й–Ӣе§ӢжҷӮгҒЁеҗҢгҒҳеҹәжә–пјҲзӣёеҜҫзҡ„SSPпјүгҒ§еҗ„еұҘиЎҢзҫ©еӢҷгҒ«й…ҚеҲҶгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮеҪ“еҲқSSPеҹәжә–пјҡProduct A = $300,000/$500,000 = 60%гҖҒService B = 40%гҖӮ$60,000еў—еҠ еҲҶгҒ®й…ҚеҲҶпјҡProduct A = $60,000 Г— 60% = $36,000гҖҒService B = $60,000 Г— 40% = $24,000гҖӮж—ўгҒ«е……и¶ігҒ—гҒҹProduct AгҒ«й…ҚеҲҶгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹ$36,000гҒҜзӣҙгҒЎгҒ«еҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳпјҲзҙҜз©ҚгӮӯгғЈгғғгғҒгӮўгғғгғ—пјүгҖҒService BгҒ®$24,000гҒҜгӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№жҸҗдҫӣгҒ«еҝңгҒҳгҒҰиӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: Which of the following transactions is within the scope of ASC 606, Revenue from Contracts with Customers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sale of finished goods by a manufacturer to a retail customer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lease income from leasing equipment to a lessee

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interest income earned on a loan receivable

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insurance contract premiums earned by an insurer

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-15-2 and 15-3, ASC 606 applies to contracts with customers to transfer goods or services in the ordinary course of business. The sale of finished goods to a customer is squarely within scope. Excluded from ASC 606 (ASC 606-10-15-2) are: lease contracts (ASC 842), insurance contracts (ASC 944), financial instruments and other contractual rights/obligations such as interest income (ASC 310/320/etc.), guarantees, and certain nonmonetary exchanges between entities in the same line of business. Leasing income, interest income, and insurance premiums fall under those other standards.

questions.stem: Orion Co. signs an agreement with a customer to deliver goods. Before either party performs and before any consideration is paid or due, each party has the unilateral enforceable right to terminate the wholly unperformed agreement without compensating the other party. Under ASC 606, how should Orion account for this agreement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No contract exists for accounting purposes; the five-step model is not applied unless the termination rights lapse or performance begins

choices.label: B
choices.text: A contract exists because both parties have signed a written agreement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Revenue should be recognized ratably over the stated agreement term

choices.label: D
choices.text: A contract exists, but all consideration must be treated as fully constrained variable consideration

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-4 states that a contract does not exist if each party has the unilateral enforceable right to terminate a wholly unperformed contract without compensating the other party. A contract is wholly unperformed when the entity has not transferred any goods or services and has not received (and is not yet entitled to receive) any consideration. A signed written document alone does not create a contract for accounting purposes if these mutual no-penalty termination rights exist. Ratable recognition over the term assumes a valid contract and a performance obligation satisfied over time, neither of which has been established. Treating the consideration as constrained variable consideration is a Step 3 concept that is irrelevant when Step 1 is not met.

questions.stem: Maple Co. determined at contract inception that all five contract-existence criteria of ASC 606 were met, including the probability of collecting substantially all of the consideration. Under ASC 606, when must Maple reassess whether the contract-existence criteria continue to be met?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the end of each reporting period, regardless of circumstances

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only if there is an indication of a significant change in facts and circumstances, such as a significant deterioration in the customer's ability to pay

choices.label: C
choices.text: Never; the assessment made at contract inception is final

choices.label: D
choices.text: Each time the customer makes a payment under the contract

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-5 requires an entity to reassess the contract-existence criteria only if there is an indication of a significant change in facts and circumstances. For example, if a customer's ability to pay deteriorates significantly, the entity reassesses whether it is probable that it will collect the consideration for the remaining goods or services. Mandatory reassessment at every reporting date is not required; the standard ties reassessment to significant changes, not to the passage of time. Saying the inception assessment is final ignores the reassessment requirement entirely, and customer payments by themselves do not trigger reassessment.

questions.subtopic: Consideration Received When No Contract Exists
questions.stem: Cedar Co. enters into an arrangement to deliver goods to a customer with poor credit, and collectibility is not probable, so the Step 1 contract criteria are not met. Cedar receives a $50,000 nonrefundable payment from the customer. Cedar has remaining obligations to transfer goods, and the arrangement has not been terminated. How should Cedar account for the $50,000 received?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize $50,000 as revenue because the payment is nonrefundable

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize revenue equal to the cost of goods delivered to date and defer the remainder

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize a $50,000 liability until the contract criteria are met or one of the specified events permitting revenue recognition occurs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disclose the receipt in the notes only, with no balance sheet recognition

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-7, when the contract criteria are not met, consideration received is recognized as revenue only when (a) the entity has no remaining obligations and substantially all consideration has been received and is nonrefundable, or (b) the contract has been terminated and the consideration received is nonrefundable, or (c) the entity has transferred control of the goods, has stopped transferring and has no obligation to transfer additional goods, and the consideration received is nonrefundable. Because Cedar still has remaining obligations and the arrangement has not been terminated, none of these events has occurred, so the $50,000 must be recorded as a liability (ASC 606-10-25-8). Nonrefundability alone does not permit revenue recognition, a cost-based partial recognition approach has no basis in the standard, and cash received always requires balance sheet recognition.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-25-7 гҒ§гҒҜгҖҒStep 1 гҒ®еҘ‘зҙ„жҲҗз«ӢиҰҒд»¶гӮ’жәҖгҒҹгҒ•гҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҸ—й ҳгҒ—гҒҹеҜҫдҫЎгӮ’еҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳгҒ§гҒҚгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜгҖҒ(a) ж®ӢеӯҳеұҘиЎҢзҫ©еӢҷгҒҢгҒӘгҒҸеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®гҒ»гҒје…ЁйЎҚгӮ’еҸ—й ҳжёҲгҒҝгҒ§иҝ”йҮ‘дёҚиҰҒгҖҒ(b) еҘ‘зҙ„гҒҢи§Јзҙ„гҒ•гӮҢеҜҫдҫЎгҒҢиҝ”йҮ‘дёҚиҰҒгҖҒ(c) иІЎгҒ®ж”Ҝй…ҚгӮ’з§»и»ўжёҲгҒҝгҒ§иҝҪеҠ з§»и»ўгҒ®зҫ©еӢҷгҒҢгҒӘгҒҸеҜҫдҫЎгҒҢиҝ”йҮ‘дёҚиҰҒгҖҒгҒ®гҒ„гҒҡгӮҢгҒӢгҒ®дәӢиұЎгҒҢз”ҹгҒҳгҒҹгҒЁгҒҚгҒ«йҷҗгӮүгӮҢгӮӢгҖӮCedar гҒ«гҒҜж®Ӣеӯҳзҫ©еӢҷгҒҢгҒӮгӮҠеҘ‘зҙ„гӮӮи§Јзҙ„гҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒгҖҒгҒ„гҒҡгӮҢгҒ«гӮӮи©ІеҪ“гҒӣгҒҡгҖҒ$50,000 гҒҜиІ еӮөгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰиЁҲдёҠгҒҷгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-25-8пјүгҖӮиҝ”йҮ‘дёҚиҰҒгҒЁгҒ„гҒҶгҒ гҒ‘гҒ§гҒҜеҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳгҒҜиӘҚгӮҒгӮүгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.stem: Birch Construction is building a facility under a long-term contract. The customer requests a change in the scope of the work, and both parties have approved the scope change, but the corresponding change in price is still under negotiation. Under ASC 606, how should Birch account for this unpriced change order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ignore the scope change until the price is finalized

choices.label: B
choices.text: Account for the additional work as a separate contract with a transaction price of zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: Suspend all revenue recognition on the contract until the negotiation is complete

choices.label: D
choices.text: Account for the change as a contract modification and estimate the change to the transaction price as variable consideration, subject to the constraint

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-11 states that a contract modification may exist even though the parties have a dispute about, or have not yet finalized, the corresponding change in price. If the scope change is approved but the price change is unpriced, the entity estimates the change to the transaction price using the variable consideration guidance, including the constraint on variable consideration. Ignoring the approved scope change would misstate the measure of progress and revenue. A separate zero-price contract is not contemplated by the modification guidance. Suspending all revenue recognition is inappropriate because the original performance obligation continues to be satisfied.

questions.subtopic: Modification Not at Standalone Selling Price - Blended Rate
questions.stem: Hudson Co. contracts to sell 120 identical units to a customer at $90 per unit ($10,800 total). After delivering 50 units, the contract is modified to add 30 more units at $60 per unit. The standalone selling price of each unit at the modification date is $75, so the additional units are not priced at their standalone selling price. The remaining units are distinct from those already delivered. What amount of revenue should Hudson recognize per unit for each unit delivered after the modification?
questions.explanation: Because the additional units are distinct but are not priced at their standalone selling price, the modification is accounted for as a termination of the original contract and creation of a new contract (ASC 606-10-25-13(b)). The consideration not yet recognized is blended over the remaining units: remaining original units 70 x $90 = $6,300, plus new units 30 x $60 = $1,800, equals $8,100 for 100 remaining units, or $81 per unit. $75 is the standalone selling price at the modification date, which would be used only to test whether the modification qualifies as a separate contract. $90 is the original per-unit price, which applies only to the 50 units already delivered. $60 is the negotiated price of the added units only and ignores the blending requirement.
questions.explanationJa: иҝҪеҠ  30 еҖӢгҒҜзӢ¬з«ӢгҒ—гҒҹиІЎгҒ гҒҢзӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јпјҲ$75пјүгҒ§дҫЎж јиЁӯе®ҡгҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒгҖҒASC 606-10-25-13(b) гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠж—§еҘ‘зҙ„гӮ’и§Јзҙ„гҒ—ж–°еҘ‘зҙ„гӮ’з· зөҗгҒ—гҒҹгӮӮгҒ®гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮжңӘиӘҚиӯҳгҒ®еҜҫдҫЎгӮ’ж®Ӣеӯҳж•°йҮҸгҒ«жҢүеҲҶгҒҷгӮӢпјҡж—§еҘ‘зҙ„ж®ӢгӮҠ 70 еҖӢГ—$90пјқ$6,300 пјӢ иҝҪеҠ  30 еҖӢГ—$60пјқ$1,800 пјқ $8,100гҖӮгҒ“гӮҢгӮ’ж®ӢгӮҠ 100 еҖӢгҒ§еүІгӮӢгҒЁ 1 еҖӢеҪ“гҒҹгӮҠ $81 гҒЁгҒӘгӮӢгҖӮ$75 гҒҜеҲҘеҘ‘зҙ„и©ІеҪ“жҖ§гҒ®еҲӨе®ҡгҒ«дҪҝгҒҶзӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгҖҒ$90 гҒҜеј•жёЎжёҲгҒҝ 50 еҖӢгҒ«гҒ®гҒҝйҒ©з”ЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢж—§еҚҳдҫЎгҖҒ$60 гҒҜжҢүеҲҶгӮ’з„ЎиҰ–гҒ—гҒҹиҝҪеҠ еҲҶгҒ®дәӨжёүдҫЎж јгҒ«гҒҷгҒҺгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.stem: A telecommunications company advertises a promotion in which customers who sign a two-year service contract receive a 'free' handset at contract inception. The handset could be sold separately and the customer can benefit from it on its own. Under ASC 606, how should the company account for the handset?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The handset is a separate performance obligation, and a portion of the total contract consideration is allocated to it based on relative standalone selling prices

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cost of the handset is recognized as a marketing expense because it is given away free

choices.label: C
choices.text: No revenue is attributed to the handset because its contractual price is zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: All contract revenue is recognized when the handset is delivered

questions.explanation: Promised goods described as 'free' are still performance obligations if they are distinct (ASC 606-10-25-16 through 25-18). The handset is capable of being distinct and is separately identifiable, so the total transaction price (the service payments over two years) is allocated between the handset and the service based on relative standalone selling prices, and the handset's allocated revenue is recognized upon delivery. Treating the handset cost as a marketing expense, or attributing zero revenue to it because of the stated contract price, ignores the allocation requirement of Step 4 вҖ” stated contract prices do not govern allocation. Recognizing all contract revenue at handset delivery would improperly accelerate the service revenue that must be recognized over the two-year period.

questions.subtopic: Criteria for a Distinct Good or Service
questions.stem: Under ASC 606, a promised good or service is distinct, and therefore a separate performance obligation, only if which of the following conditions are met?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The good or service has a separately stated price in the contract

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity regularly sells the good or service on a standalone basis

choices.label: C
choices.text: The customer can benefit from the good or service on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract

choices.label: D
choices.text: The good or service is delivered at a different time than the other goods or services in the contract

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-19 establishes two criteria, both of which must be met for a good or service to be distinct: (1) it is capable of being distinct вҖ” the customer can benefit from it on its own or together with other readily available resources, and (2) it is distinct within the context of the contract вҖ” the promise is separately identifiable from other promises. A separately stated contract price is not determinative; pricing format does not drive the unit of account. Regular standalone sales are an indicator that a customer can benefit from the good on its own, but it is not the stated requirement by itself. Different delivery timing does not make a promise distinct; for example, design and construction services delivered at different times may still be combined when they are highly interdependent.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-25-19 гҒҜгҖҒиІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒҢгҖҢзӢ¬з«ӢгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢпјҲdistinctпјүгҖҚгҒҹгӮҒгҒ® 2 иҰҒд»¶гӮ’е®ҡгӮҒгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢпјҡ(1) йЎ§е®ўгҒҢгҒқгҒ®иІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№еҚҳзӢ¬гҒ§гҖҒгҒҫгҒҹгҒҜе®№жҳ“гҒ«е…ҘжүӢгҒ§гҒҚгӮӢд»–гҒ®иіҮжәҗгҒЁзө„гҒҝеҗҲгӮҸгҒӣгҒҰдҫҝзӣҠгӮ’еҫ—гӮүгӮҢгӮӢгҒ“гҒЁпјҲcapable of being distinctпјүгҖҒ(2) еҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®иҰізӮ№гҒ«гҒҠгҒ„гҒҰд»–гҒ®зҙ„жқҹгҒЁеҢәеҲҶгҒ—гҒҰиӯҳеҲҘгҒ§гҒҚгӮӢгҒ“гҒЁпјҲseparately identifiableпјүгҖӮдёЎж–№гӮ’жәҖгҒҹгҒҷеҝ…иҰҒгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„дёҠгҒ®дҫЎж јгҒ®еҢәеҲҶиЎЁзӨәгҒҜжұәе®ҡиҰҒеӣ гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҒеҚҳзӢ¬иІ©еЈІгҒ®е®ҹзёҫгҒҜиҰҒд»¶ (1) гҒ®жҢҮжЁҷгҒ«гҒҷгҒҺгҒҡгҖҒеј•жёЎжҷӮжңҹгҒ®зӣёйҒ•гӮӮзӢ¬з«ӢжҖ§гӮ’жұәе®ҡгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.stem: Sterling Co. enters into a contract with a fixed price of $600,000 plus a performance bonus that depends on the completion date. Sterling estimates a 50% probability of earning a $100,000 bonus, a 30% probability of earning a $50,000 bonus, and a 20% probability of earning no bonus. Sterling concludes the expected value method best predicts the consideration and that no constraint is necessary. What is the transaction price?
questions.explanation: Under the expected value method (ASC 606-10-32-8(a)), the variable consideration is the probability-weighted sum of the possible outcomes: (50% x $100,000) + (30% x $50,000) + (20% x $0) = $50,000 + $15,000 + $0 = $65,000. The transaction price is $600,000 + $65,000 = $665,000. $600,000 omits the bonus entirely, which would be appropriate only if the bonus were fully constrained. $615,000 results from weighting only the $50,000 outcome and ignoring the $100,000 outcome. $700,000 assumes the maximum bonus is earned, which is the most optimistic single outcome rather than a probability-weighted estimate.

questions.stem: Lakeside Hospital provides emergency services to uninsured patients at a standard rate of $20,000 per procedure. Based on extensive historical experience with this class of patients, Lakeside expects to collect, and intends to pursue, only 30% of the standard rate, although it bills the full amount. The Step 1 criteria are met based on the expected amount. What amount of revenue should Lakeside recognize per procedure when service is provided?

choices.label: B
choices.text: $20,000, with $14,000 of bad debt expense recognized at the same time

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0 until cash is collected

questions.explanation: Lakeside's history of accepting less than the billed rate from this patient class indicates an implicit price concession, which is variable consideration under ASC 606-10-32-7. The transaction price is the amount the entity expects to be entitled to вҖ” $20,000 x 30% = $6,000 вҖ” not the standard billed rate. Recognizing $20,000 of revenue with simultaneous bad debt expense reflects the pre-ASC 606 gross approach and overstates both revenue and expense; under ASC 606 the expected concession reduces the transaction price rather than creating a day-one credit loss. $14,000 is the expected concession (the amount not collected), not the expected collection. Waiting for cash is unnecessary because the Step 1 criteria are met at the $6,000 expected amount.
questions.explanationJa: и«ӢжұӮйЎҚгӮҲгӮҠе°‘гҒӘгҒ„йҮ‘йЎҚгӮ’жӯҙеҸІзҡ„гҒ«еҸ—гҒ‘е…ҘгӮҢгҒҰгҒҚгҒҹе®ҹзёҫгҒҜгҖҢй»ҷзӨәзҡ„гҒӘдҫЎж јиӯІжӯ©пјҲimplicit price concessionпјүгҖҚгӮ’зӨәгҒ—гҖҒгҒ“гӮҢгҒҜеӨүеӢ•еҜҫдҫЎгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-32-7пјүгҖӮеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒҜжЁ©еҲ©гӮ’еҫ—гӮӢгҒЁиҰӢиҫјгӮҖйҮ‘йЎҚгҖҒгҒҷгҒӘгӮҸгҒЎ $20,000Г—30%пјқ$6,000 гҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒжЁҷжә–и«ӢжұӮйЎҚгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ$20,000 гӮ’еҸҺзӣҠиЁҲдёҠгҒ—еҗҢжҷӮгҒ« $14,000 гҒ®иІёеҖ’иІ»з”ЁгӮ’иЁҲдёҠгҒҷгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜ ASC 606 д»ҘеүҚгҒ®гӮ°гғӯгӮ№еҮҰзҗҶгҒ§гҖҒеҸҺзӣҠгҒЁиІ»з”ЁгҒ®еҸҢж–№гӮ’йҒҺеӨ§иЎЁзӨәгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ$14,000 гҒҜиӯІжӯ©йЎҚпјҲжңӘеӣһеҸҺиҰӢиҫјеҲҶпјүгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠеҸҺзӣҠгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮStep 1 гҒҜ $6,000 гғҷгғјгӮ№гҒ§жәҖгҒҹгҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒҹгӮҒзҸҫйҮ‘еӣһеҸҺгҒҫгҒ§еҫ…гҒӨеҝ…иҰҒгӮӮгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Significant Financing - Accretion on Advance Payment
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Granite Co. receives a $200,000 advance payment from a customer for specialized equipment that will be delivered on December 31, Year 2. The contract contains a significant financing component, and the appropriate discount rate is 5%. What amount of revenue should Granite recognize when the equipment is delivered on December 31, Year 2?
questions.explanation: When the customer pays significantly in advance, the entity is effectively borrowing from the customer. The contract liability is accreted using the discount rate, with the accretion recognized as interest expense, and revenue at delivery equals the accreted balance: $200,000 x 1.05 x 1.05 = $220,500. Granite recognizes interest expense of $10,000 in Year 1 and $10,500 in Year 2. $200,000 ignores the significant financing component. $210,000 accretes interest for only one year instead of two. $220,000 uses simple interest ($10,000 x 2) instead of compounding the contract liability balance.
questions.explanationJa: йЎ§е®ўгҒҢеӨ§е№…гҒ«еүҚжү•гҒ„гҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒдјҒжҘӯгҒҜе®ҹиіӘзҡ„гҒ«йЎ§е®ўгҒӢгӮүеҖҹе…ҘгӮ’иЎҢгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒҜеүІеј•зҺҮгҒ§еҲ©жҒҜгӮ’д»ҳгҒ—гҒҰеў—йЎҚпјҲaccretionпјүгҒ—гҖҒгҒқгҒ®еў—еҠ еҲҶгҒҜж”Ҝжү•еҲ©жҒҜгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰиЁҲдёҠгҖҒеј•жёЎжҷӮгҒ®еҸҺзӣҠгҒҜеў—йЎҚеҫҢгҒ®ж®Ӣй«ҳгҒЁгҒӘгӮӢпјҡ$200,000Г—1.05Г—1.05пјқ$220,500пјҲYear 1 еҲ©жҒҜ $10,000гҖҒYear 2 еҲ©жҒҜ $10,500пјүгҖӮ$200,000 гҒҜйҮҚиҰҒгҒӘйҮ‘иһҚиҰҒзҙ гҒ®з„ЎиҰ–гҖҒ$210,000 гҒҜ 1 е№ҙеҲҶгҒ®гҒҝгҒ®д»ҳеҲ©гҖҒ$220,000 гҒҜиӨҮеҲ©гҒ§гҒӘгҒҸеҚҳеҲ©пјҲ$10,000Г—2пјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠпјҲASC 606-10-32-15гҖң32-20пјүгҖӮ

questions.stem: Fulton Co. agrees to provide engineering services to a startup customer in exchange for 1,000 shares of the customer's common stock. At contract inception, the shares have a fair value of $50 per share. When the services are completed and the shares are transferred to Fulton three months later, the shares have a fair value of $58 per share. What amount of service revenue should Fulton recognize?

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0 until the shares are sold for cash

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-21 through 32-23, noncash consideration is measured at its fair value at contract inception: 1,000 shares x $50 = $50,000. Changes in the fair value of the shares after inception that are due to the form of the consideration (changes in the stock price) are not included in the transaction price; any subsequent gain on the shares is accounted for under other guidance (for example, ASC 321) after the shares are received. $58,000 incorrectly measures the consideration at the transfer date. $54,000 is an unsupported average of the two values. Deferring all revenue until the shares are converted to cash has no basis вҖ” receipt of noncash consideration with a measurable fair value supports revenue recognition.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-32-21гҖң32-23 гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒзҸҫйҮ‘д»ҘеӨ–гҒ®еҜҫдҫЎгҒҜеҘ‘зҙ„й–Ӣе§ӢжҷӮгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒ§жё¬е®ҡгҒҷгӮӢпјҡ1,000 ж ӘГ—$50пјқ$50,000гҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„й–Ӣе§ӢеҫҢгҒ®ж ӘдҫЎеӨүеӢ•пјҲеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®еҪўж…ӢгҒ«иө·еӣ гҒҷгӮӢе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒ®еӨүеӢ•пјүгҒҜеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒ«еҗ«гӮҒгҒҡгҖҒеҸ—й ҳеҫҢгҒ®ж ӘејҸгҒ®и©•дҫЎжҗҚзӣҠгҒҜ ASC 321 зӯүгҒ®д»–гҒ®еҹәжә–гҒ§еҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ$58,000 гҒҜз§»и»ўж—Ҙжё¬е®ҡгҒ®иӘӨгӮҠгҖҒ$54,000 гҒҜж №жӢ гҒ®гҒӘгҒ„е№іеқҮеҖӨгҖҒзҸҫйҮ‘еҢ–гҒҫгҒ§еҸҺзӣҠгӮ’з№°гӮҠе»¶гҒ№гӮӢеҮҰзҗҶгҒ«гӮӮж №жӢ гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Consideration Payable to Customer - Payment Exceeding Fair Value of Service
questions.stem: Walton Co., a manufacturer, sells goods to a retailer for $400,000 during the year. Walton also pays the retailer $30,000 for an advertising campaign featuring Walton's products. The advertising is a distinct service, and its fair value is reliably determined to be $18,000. What amount of revenue should Walton recognize from sales to this retailer?
