questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-26, consideration payable to a customer in exchange for a distinct good or service is accounted for as a purchase, but only up to the fair value of that good or service. Any excess of the payment over the fair value reduces the transaction price. Here, $18,000 is recognized as advertising expense and the excess of $30,000 - $18,000 = $12,000 reduces revenue: $400,000 - $12,000 = $388,000. $400,000 ignores the excess payment. $382,000 incorrectly reduces revenue by the $18,000 fair value rather than the excess. $370,000 incorrectly treats the entire $30,000 payment as a reduction of revenue, which would be correct only if the advertising were not a distinct service or its fair value could not be estimated.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-32-26 гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒ«ж”Ҝжү•гӮҸгӮҢгӮӢеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®гҒҶгҒЎгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒӢгӮүеҸ—гҒ‘еҸ–гӮӢзӢ¬з«ӢгҒ—гҒҹиІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒҫгҒ§гҒҜд»•е…ҘпјҲиІ»з”ЁпјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒ—гҖҒе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгӮ’и¶…йҒҺгҒҷгӮӢйғЁеҲҶгҒҜеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒ®жёӣйЎҚгҒЁгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮеәғе‘ҠиІ» $18,000 гӮ’иІ»з”ЁиЁҲдёҠгҒ—гҖҒи¶…йҒҺйЎҚ $30,000вҲ’$18,000пјқ$12,000 гӮ’еҸҺзӣҠгҒӢгӮүжҺ§йҷӨпјҡ$400,000вҲ’$12,000пјқ$388,000гҖӮ$400,000 гҒҜи¶…йҒҺйЎҚгҒ®з„ЎиҰ–гҖҒ$382,000 гҒҜи¶…йҒҺйЎҚгҒ§гҒӘгҒҸе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨеҲҶгӮ’жҺ§йҷӨгҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠгҖҒ$370,000 гҒҜе…ЁйЎҚ $30,000 гӮ’еҸҺзӣҠжёӣйЎҚгҒЁгҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠпјҲгӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒҢзӢ¬з«ӢгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгӮ„е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгӮ’иҰӢз©ҚгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгҒ®еҮҰзҗҶпјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Allocating a Discount to Fewer Than All Performance Obligations
questions.stem: Under ASC 606, an entity may allocate a discount entirely to one or more, but not all, performance obligations in a contract only if certain criteria are met. Which of the following is NOT one of those criteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The discount is stated as a separate line item in the contract

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity regularly sells each distinct good or service in the contract on a standalone basis

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity regularly sells, on a standalone basis, a bundle of some of those goods or services at a discount

choices.label: D
choices.text: The discount attributable to that bundle is substantially the same as the discount in the contract, and an analysis provides observable evidence of the obligations to which the discount belongs

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-32-37 permits allocating a discount to fewer than all performance obligations only when three criteria are met: the entity regularly sells each distinct good or service on a standalone basis; the entity also regularly sells, on a standalone basis, a bundle of some of those goods or services at a discount to their standalone selling prices; and the discount attributable to that bundle is substantially the same as the contract's discount, with observable evidence linking the discount to specific obligations. How the discount is presented in the contract document вҖ” including whether it appears as a separate line item вҖ” is irrelevant; the allocation is driven by observable standalone and bundle pricing, not by contract drafting.

questions.subtopic: Relative Standalone Selling Price Allocation - Three Items
questions.stem: Pinnacle Co. enters into a contract with a customer for a total price of $270,000 covering three distinct performance obligations with the following observable standalone selling prices: Product X, $150,000; Product Y, $90,000; and Product Z, $60,000. The discount does not relate to any specific obligation. What amount of the transaction price should be allocated to Product X?
questions.explanation: The total standalone selling prices are $150,000 + $90,000 + $60,000 = $300,000, but the contract price is $270,000, so the $30,000 discount is allocated proportionately (ASC 606-10-32-31 and 32-36). Product X receives $270,000 x ($150,000 / $300,000) = $135,000. $150,000 is Product X's full standalone selling price, which ignores the proportionate discount. $120,000 does not correspond to a relative-SSP computation for X. $90,000 is Product Y's standalone selling price, not an allocation to X.
questions.explanationJa: зӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгҒ®еҗҲиЁҲгҒҜ $150,000пјӢ$90,000пјӢ$60,000пјқ$300,000 гҒ гҒҢеҘ‘зҙ„дҫЎж јгҒҜ $270,000 гҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒ$30,000 гҒ®еҖӨеј•гҒҚгӮ’жҜ”дҫӢй…ҚеҲҶгҒҷгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-32-31гҖҒ32-36пјүгҖӮProduct X гҒёгҒ®й…ҚеҲҶйЎҚгҒҜ $270,000Г—(150,000/300,000)пјқ$135,000гҖӮ$150,000 гҒҜеҖӨеј•гҒҚгӮ’з„ЎиҰ–гҒ—гҒҹ X гҒ®зӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгҒқгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҖҒ$90,000 гҒҜ Y гҒ®зӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒгҒ„гҒҡгӮҢгӮӮй…ҚеҲҶиЁҲз®—гҒ«гҒӘгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, an entity may use the residual approach to estimate the standalone selling price of a good or service only in limited circumstances. Which of the following circumstances permits use of the residual approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity wants to allocate the entire contract discount to that good or service

choices.label: B
choices.text: The good or service is the most significant deliverable in the contract

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity sells the same good or service to different customers at highly variable prices, or has not yet established a price for it and it has not previously been sold on a standalone basis

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cost of the good or service cannot be reliably measured

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-32-34(c) permits the residual approach only when the standalone selling price is highly variable (the entity sells the same good or service to different customers at or near the same time for a broad range of amounts) or highly uncertain (the entity has not yet established a price and the good or service has not previously been sold on a standalone basis). A desire to allocate the discount to a particular item is not a basis for the method, and the relative significance of the deliverable is irrelevant. Cost measurement difficulties relate to the expected-cost-plus-margin approach, not to the residual approach, which works backward from the total transaction price minus observable standalone selling prices of the other goods and services.

questions.subtopic: Enforceable Right to Payment for Performance to Date
questions.stem: Keystone Co. manufactures an asset with no alternative use and is evaluating whether it has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date, which would require over-time revenue recognition. Under ASC 606, which of the following payment terms satisfies the right-to-payment criterion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The customer forfeits a 10% deposit if it cancels the contract

choices.label: B
choices.text: Upon customer termination, Keystone is entitled to reimbursement of costs incurred to date only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keystone is entitled to full payment only upon completion and delivery of the asset

choices.label: D
choices.text: Upon customer termination for reasons other than Keystone's nonperformance, Keystone is entitled at all times to an amount that compensates it for costs incurred plus a reasonable profit margin on performance completed to date

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-29 and 606-10-55-11 require that, at all times throughout the contract, the entity be entitled to an amount that at least compensates it for performance completed to date if the customer terminates for reasons other than the entity's failure to perform. Compensation for performance to date means recovery of costs incurred plus a reasonable profit margin (ASC 606-10-55-11), not merely reimbursement of costs, which compensates only for the resources consumed. A forfeitable deposit of a fixed percentage does not necessarily approximate the value of work completed at every point during the contract. Payment contingent solely on completion fails the 'at all times' requirement entirely.

questions.stem: Summit Builders has a $6,000,000 fixed-price construction contract accounted for over time using the cost-to-cost input method. In Year 1, costs incurred were $1,500,000 and estimated total costs were $5,000,000. At the end of Year 2, cumulative costs incurred were $3,780,000 and estimated total costs were revised to $5,400,000. What gross profit should Summit recognize in Year 2?
questions.explanation: Year 1: progress = $1,500,000 / $5,000,000 = 30%; revenue = $1,800,000; gross profit = $1,800,000 - $1,500,000 = $300,000. Year 2 cumulative: progress = $3,780,000 / $5,400,000 = 70%; cumulative revenue = $6,000,000 x 70% = $4,200,000; cumulative gross profit = $4,200,000 - $3,780,000 = $420,000. Year 2 gross profit = $420,000 - $300,000 = $120,000. $300,000 is the Year 1 gross profit. $420,000 is the cumulative (inception-to-date) gross profit, not the Year 2 amount. $600,000 is the total expected gross profit on the contract ($6,000,000 - $5,400,000), which will be recognized only over the full contract life.

questions.stem: Crestview Construction has a $4,000,000 fixed-price contract recognized over time using the cost-to-cost method. In Year 1, costs incurred were $1,200,000, estimated total costs were $3,000,000, and Crestview recognized $400,000 of gross profit. At the end of Year 2, estimated total costs are revised to $4,400,000, indicating an overall loss on the contract. What amount of loss should Crestview recognize in Year 2?

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0, because losses are recognized only when the contract is complete

questions.explanation: When a contract accounted for over time becomes a loss contract, the entire anticipated loss is recognized immediately (ASC 605-35-25 onerous-contract guidance retained for construction-type contracts). The total anticipated loss is $4,000,000 - $4,400,000 = $(400,000). Because $400,000 of gross profit was already recognized in Year 1, the Year 2 charge must both eliminate that profit and record the full anticipated loss: $400,000 + $400,000 = $800,000 loss in Year 2. $400,000 reflects only the overall contract loss without reversing the previously recognized profit. $1,200,000 has no computational basis in these facts. Deferring the loss to completion violates the requirement to recognize the full expected loss in the period it becomes evident.

questions.stem: Railway Services Co. contracts to refurbish 60 identical rail cars for a total fixed price of $900,000. The performance obligation is satisfied over time, and Railway uses an output method based on units completed and delivered, which faithfully depicts its performance. By year-end, 24 rail cars have been completed and delivered to the customer. What amount of revenue should Railway recognize for the year?
questions.explanation: Under an output method, progress is measured by the value of the units transferred relative to the total units promised: 24 / 60 = 40% complete. Revenue = $900,000 x 40% = $360,000 (ASC 606-10-55-17). $240,000 incorrectly multiplies the unit count by an unsupported rate. $300,000 would correspond to one-third completion (20 of 60 cars), which is not the case. $450,000 assumes 50% completion (30 cars), overstating the units actually delivered.

questions.stem: Apex Staffing provides temporary personnel to a client under a three-year contract and bills the client $150 per hour worked, an amount that corresponds directly with the value to the client of the services performed to date. The performance obligation is satisfied over time. Under ASC 606, how may Apex measure progress and recognize revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apex must estimate total expected hours over three years and apply a cost-to-cost percentage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apex must recognize the estimated total contract value on a straight-line basis over three years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apex must defer all revenue until the total number of hours is known

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apex may use the practical expedient to recognize revenue in the amount it has the right to invoice each period

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-55-18 provides a practical expedient: if the entity's right to consideration corresponds directly with the value to the customer of performance completed to date (such as a fixed amount per hour of service), the entity may recognize revenue in the amount to which it has the right to invoice. Estimating total hours for a cost-to-cost calculation is unnecessary when the invoice amount directly tracks value delivered. Straight-line recognition of an estimated total would not reflect actual hours worked each period. Deferring revenue until total hours are known contradicts over-time recognition entirely. An entity using this expedient also need not disclose remaining performance obligations for the contract (ASC 606-10-50-14).

questions.stem: Harbor Co. operates an e-commerce site. For one product line, Harbor purchases the goods from the manufacturer, takes title and inventory risk before customer orders are received, sets the selling price, and is primarily responsible for fulfilling orders. During the period, Harbor purchased 200 units at $70 each and sold all 200 units at $100 each. What amount of revenue should Harbor recognize for this product line?
questions.explanation: Harbor controls the goods before they are transferred to customers вҖ” it takes title and inventory risk before sale, sets prices, and is primarily responsible for fulfillment вҖ” so Harbor is a principal under ASC 606-10-55-37. A principal recognizes revenue at the gross amount of consideration: 200 x $100 = $20,000, with cost of goods sold of 200 x $70 = $14,000 presented separately. $6,000 is the net margin (200 x $30), which would be the agent presentation. $14,000 is the cost of the goods, not revenue. $26,000 improperly adds the net margin to the gross sales amount.
questions.explanationJa: Harbor гҒҜиІ©еЈІеүҚгҒ«жүҖжңүжЁ©гҒЁеңЁеә«гғӘгӮ№гӮҜгӮ’еј•гҒҚеҸ—гҒ‘гҖҒдҫЎж јгӮ’иЁӯе®ҡгҒ—гҖҒеұҘиЎҢгҒ«дё»гҒҹгӮӢиІ¬д»»гӮ’иІ гҒҶгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒёгҒ®з§»и»ўеүҚгҒ«иІЎгӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҒ—гҒҰгҒҠгӮҠжң¬дәәпјҲprincipalпјүгҒ«и©ІеҪ“гҒҷгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-55-37пјүгҖӮжң¬дәәгҒҜеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®з·ҸйЎҚгҒ§еҸҺзӣҠгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢпјҡ200Г—$100пјқ$20,000пјҲеЈІдёҠеҺҹдҫЎ 200Г—$70пјқ$14,000 гҒҜеҲҘйҖ”иЎЁзӨәпјүгҖӮ$6,000 гҒҜзҙ”йЎҚгғһгғјгӮёгғіпјҲд»ЈзҗҶдәәгҒ®е ҙеҗҲгҒ®иЎЁзӨәпјүгҖҒ$14,000 гҒҜеҺҹдҫЎгҖҒ$26,000 гҒҜз·ҸйЎҚгҒ«гғһгғјгӮёгғігӮ’еҠ з®—гҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: Brandwell Co. grants a franchisee the right to use its trade name for 10 years for an upfront fixed fee of $600,000, paid in full at inception. The trade name is symbolic intellectual property, and the license period begins on April 1, Year 1. Brandwell recognizes revenue on a straight-line basis over the license period. What amount of revenue should Brandwell recognize in Year 1 (calendar year)?
questions.explanation: A license of symbolic IP (such as a brand or trade name) provides a right to access the entity's intellectual property, so revenue is recognized over the license period (ASC 606-10-55-58 through 55-60). The annual amount is $600,000 / 10 years = $60,000, and the license was in effect for 9 months of Year 1 (April through December): $60,000 x 9/12 = $45,000. $15,000 corresponds to only 3 months. $60,000 assumes a full year of access. $600,000 treats the license as functional IP recognized at a point in time, which contradicts the symbolic nature of a trade name.
questions.explanationJa: гғ–гғ©гғігғүгӮ„е•ҶеҸ·гҒӘгҒ©гҒ®иұЎеҫҙзҡ„зҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈпјҲsymbolic IPпјүгҒ®гғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№гҒҜгҖҒзҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈгҒёгҒ®гҖҢгӮўгӮҜгӮ»гӮ№жЁ©гҖҚгӮ’жҸҗдҫӣгҒҷгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒгғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№жңҹй–“гҒ«гӮҸгҒҹгӮҠеҸҺзӣҠгӮ’иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-55-58гҖң55-60пјүгҖӮе№ҙйЎҚпјқ$600,000Г·10 е№ҙпјқ$60,000гҖҒYear 1 гҒ®гғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№жңүеҠ№жңҹй–“гҒҜ 4 жңҲгҖң12 жңҲгҒ® 9 гҒӢжңҲпјҡ$60,000Г—9/12пјқ$45,000гҖӮ$15,000 гҒҜ 3 гҒӢжңҲеҲҶгҒ®гҒҝгҖҒ$60,000 гҒҜйҖҡе№ҙеҲҶгҖҒ$600,000 гҒҜж©ҹиғҪзҡ„зҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰдёҖжҷӮзӮ№гҒ§иӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒҹе ҙеҗҲгҒ®иӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: On December 20, Year 1, Softline Co. delivers the activation code for a software license (functional intellectual property, a distinct right-to-use license) to a customer. The contract states that the license period begins on January 1, Year 2. When may Softline recognize the license revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: December 20, Year 1, when the activation code is delivered

choices.label: B
choices.text: On the contract signing date

choices.label: C
choices.text: January 1, Year 2, when the license period begins and the customer can use and benefit from the license

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ratably over Year 2

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-55-58C prohibits recognizing revenue from a license before both (1) the entity has provided or otherwise made available a copy of the IP to the customer, and (2) the beginning of the period during which the customer is able to use and benefit from the license. Although the code was delivered on December 20, the customer cannot use or benefit from the license until January 1, Year 2, so revenue is recognized on that date. Delivery of the code alone, or contract signing, is too early. Ratable recognition over Year 2 applies to right-to-access (symbolic IP) licenses or licenses recognized over time, not to a right-to-use license of functional IP, which is recognized at the point in time the license period begins.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-55-58C гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒгғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№еҸҺзӣҠгҒҜ (1) зҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈгҒ®иӨҮиЈҪзү©гӮ’йЎ§е®ўгҒ«жҸҗдҫӣжёҲгҒҝгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҖҒгҒӢгҒӨ (2) йЎ§е®ўгҒҢгғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№гӮ’дҪҝз”ЁгҒ—дҫҝзӣҠгӮ’еҫ—гӮүгӮҢгӮӢжңҹй–“гҒҢй–Ӣе§ӢгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҖҒгҒ®дёЎж–№гӮ’жәҖгҒҹгҒҷгҒҫгҒ§иӘҚиӯҳгҒ§гҒҚгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮгӮўгӮҜгғҶгӮЈгғҷгғјгӮ·гғ§гғігӮігғјгғүгҒҜ 12 жңҲ 20 ж—ҘгҒ«еј•гҒҚжёЎгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹгҒҢгҖҒйЎ§е®ўгҒҢдҪҝз”ЁгҒ§гҒҚгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜ Year 2 гҒ® 1 жңҲ 1 ж—ҘгҒӢгӮүгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒгҒқгҒ®ж—ҘгҒ«еҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮгӮігғјгғүеј•жёЎж—ҘгӮ„еҘ‘зҙ„з· зөҗж—ҘгҒ§гҒҜж—©гҒҷгҒҺгӮӢгҖӮYear 2 гҒ«гӮҸгҒҹгӮӢжҢүеҲҶиӘҚиӯҳгҒҜиұЎеҫҙзҡ„зҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈпјҲгӮўгӮҜгӮ»гӮ№жЁ©пјүгҒ®еҮҰзҗҶгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒж©ҹиғҪзҡ„зҹҘзҡ„иІЎз”ЈгҒ®дҪҝз”ЁжЁ©гғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№гҒҜгғ©гӮӨгӮ»гғігӮ№жңҹй–“й–Ӣе§ӢжҷӮзӮ№гҒ§дёҖжҷӮгҒ«иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.stem: Imagecorp licenses its brand (symbolic intellectual property) to a licensee for five years. The contract requires a sales-based royalty of 4% of the licensee's sales, subject to a cumulative minimum guarantee of $1,000,000 over the license term. How should Imagecorp recognize revenue from this arrangement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize the entire $1,000,000 minimum at contract inception and royalties as billed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize all amounts, including the minimum, only as the licensee's sales occur

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize no revenue until cumulative royalties exceed $1,000,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize the fixed $1,000,000 minimum over the license period using an appropriate measure of progress, and recognize royalties in excess of the minimum as the underlying sales occur

questions.explanation: A minimum guarantee in a sales-based royalty arrangement is fixed consideration, not variable consideration, because the entity is entitled to it regardless of the licensee's sales. For symbolic IP recognized over time, the fixed minimum is recognized over the license period using an appropriate measure of progress, while royalties expected to exceed the minimum are subject to the sales-based royalty exception (ASC 606-10-55-65) and recognized as the licensee's subsequent sales occur. Recognizing the full minimum at inception ignores the over-time nature of a symbolic IP license. Recognizing everything only as sales occur misapplies the royalty exception to the fixed portion. Waiting until royalties exceed the minimum defers fixed consideration that is being earned as access is provided.

questions.subtopic: Right of Return - Revenue Net of Expected Returns
questions.stem: Norfield Co. sells 4,000 units of a product at $25 per unit in December with a right of return. Based on reliable historical experience, Norfield expects 6% of the units to be returned, and the expected value method best predicts the returns. What amount of revenue should Norfield recognize at the time of sale?
questions.explanation: Sales with a right of return are accounted for by recognizing revenue only for the goods not expected to be returned (ASC 606-10-55-23). Gross sales are 4,000 x $25 = $100,000; expected returns are 6% x $100,000 = $6,000. Revenue = $100,000 - $6,000 = $94,000, with a $6,000 refund liability recognized for the expected returns. $100,000 ignores the expected returns. $88,000 incorrectly doubles the return percentage. $6,000 is the refund liability, not the revenue.

questions.subtopic: Combined Assurance and Service-Type Warranties - Liabilities at Sale
questions.stem: Voltway Co. sells 60 power generators for $2,000 each ($120,000 total) during December. Each generator carries a standard two-year assurance-type warranty, with estimated warranty costs of $80 per unit. In addition, customers separately purchased extended service-type warranty contracts totaling $9,000, covering years 3 and 4. No warranty claims have been incurred and no extended-warranty service has been provided as of year-end. What total amount of warranty-related liabilities should Voltway report at year-end?
questions.explanation: The assurance-type warranty is not a performance obligation; it is accrued as a cost liability under ASC 460 at the time of sale: 60 x $80 = $4,800. The separately priced service-type warranty is a distinct performance obligation, so the $9,000 received is a contract liability (deferred revenue) until the coverage period begins and revenue is recognized. Total warranty-related liabilities = $4,800 + $9,000 = $13,800. $4,800 omits the deferred service-warranty revenue. $9,000 omits the assurance-warranty accrual. Reporting no liability ignores both obligations.
questions.explanationJa: дҝқиЁјеһӢпјҲassurance-typeпјүдҝқиЁјгҒҜеұҘиЎҢзҫ©еӢҷгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҒиІ©еЈІжҷӮгҒ« ASC 460 гҒ«еҹәгҒҘгҒҚиІ»з”ЁгҒЁдҝқиЁјеј•еҪ“йҮ‘гӮ’иЁҲдёҠгҒҷгӮӢпјҡ60Г—$80пјқ$4,800гҖӮдёҖж–№гҖҒеҲҘйҖ”иІ©еЈІгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹжңүе„ҹгҒ®гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№еһӢпјҲservice-typeпјүе»¶й•·дҝқиЁјгҒҜзӢ¬з«ӢгҒ—гҒҹеұҘиЎҢзҫ©еӢҷгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒеҸ—й ҳгҒ—гҒҹ $9,000 гҒҜдҝқиЁјжңҹй–“й–Ӣе§ӢгҒҫгҒ§еҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөпјҲеүҚеҸ—еҸҺзӣҠпјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰз№°гӮҠе»¶гҒ№гӮӢгҖӮе№ҙеәҰжң«гҒ®дҝқиЁјй–ўйҖЈиІ еӮөеҗҲиЁҲпјқ$4,800пјӢ$9,000пјқ$13,800гҖӮ$4,800 гҒҜеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒ®и„ұжјҸгҖҒ$9,000 гҒҜдҝқиЁјеј•еҪ“йҮ‘гҒ®и„ұжјҸгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Contract Liability When Billings Exceed Revenue
questions.stem: Meridian Co. has a single contract with a customer. As of year-end: amounts billed to the customer (unconditional right to payment) total $500,000, revenue recognized to date totals $440,000, and cash collected totals $410,000. What contract liability, if any, should Meridian report at year-end?
questions.explanation: A contract liability arises when the entity has billed or received consideration before transferring the related goods or services. Because billings ($500,000) exceed revenue recognized ($440,000) by $60,000, Meridian has an obligation to perform for which it has already become entitled to consideration, so it reports a $60,000 contract liability. Separately, the $90,000 of billed but uncollected amounts ($500,000 - $410,000) is a receivable because the right to payment is unconditional вҖ” it is not the contract liability. $30,000 is revenue recognized minus cash collected, a figure that mixes the receivable and liability concepts. Reporting no liability is wrong because the customer has been billed for more than has been earned.
questions.explanationJa: еҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒҜгҖҒй–ўйҖЈгҒҷгӮӢиІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒ®з§»и»ўеүҚгҒ«еҜҫдҫЎгӮ’и«ӢжұӮжёҲгҒҝгҒҫгҒҹгҒҜеҸ—й ҳжёҲгҒҝгҒ®е ҙеҗҲгҒ«з”ҹгҒҳгӮӢгҖӮи«ӢжұӮйЎҚ $500,000 гҒҢиӘҚиӯҳжёҲгҒҝеҸҺзӣҠ $440,000 гӮ’ $60,000 дёҠеӣһгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮө $60,000 гӮ’иЁҲдёҠгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮи«ӢжұӮжёҲгҒҝжңӘеӣһеҸҺйЎҚ $90,000пјҲ$500,000вҲ’$410,000пјүгҒҜж”Ҝжү•гҒёгҒ®жЁ©еҲ©гҒҢз„ЎжқЎд»¶гҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒеӮөжЁ©пјҲreceivableпјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҲҘйҖ”иЁҲдёҠгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ$30,000пјҲеҸҺзӣҠвҲ’еӣһеҸҺйЎҚпјүгҒҜеӮөжЁ©гҒЁиІ еӮөгҒ®жҰӮеҝөгӮ’ж··еҗҢгҒ—гҒҹйҮ‘йЎҚгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-45-2пјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Contract Liability - Service Contract Spanning Year-End
questions.stem: On September 1, Year 1, Clearview Co. receives $96,000 in advance for a 12-month equipment monitoring service beginning on that date. The service is a single performance obligation satisfied evenly over time. What contract liability should Clearview report on its December 31, Year 1 balance sheet?
questions.explanation: From September 1 through December 31, four months of the 12-month service have been provided, so revenue recognized is $96,000 x 4/12 = $32,000. The remaining contract liability is $96,000 - $32,000 = $64,000, representing the eight months of service still to be performed. $96,000 ignores the four months already performed. $32,000 is the revenue recognized, not the remaining liability. $88,000 incorrectly assumes only one month of service has been provided.
questions.explanationJa: 9 жңҲ 1 ж—ҘгҒӢгӮү 12 жңҲ 31 ж—ҘгҒҫгҒ§гҒ® 4 гҒӢжңҲеҲҶгҒ®гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒҢжҸҗдҫӣжёҲгҒҝгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒиӘҚиӯҳжёҲгҒҝеҸҺзӣҠпјқ$96,000Г—4/12пјқ$32,000гҖӮж®ӢгӮҠгҒ®еҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөпјқ$96,000вҲ’$32,000пјқ$64,000пјҲжңӘжҸҗдҫӣгҒ® 8 гҒӢжңҲеҲҶпјүгҖӮ$96,000 гҒҜеұҘиЎҢжёҲгҒҝ 4 гҒӢжңҲгҒ®з„ЎиҰ–гҖҒ$32,000 гҒҜеҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳйЎҚгҒ§гҒӮгҒЈгҒҰиІ еӮөж®Ӣй«ҳгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҒ$88,000 гҒҜ 1 гҒӢжңҲеҲҶгҒ—гҒӢеұҘиЎҢгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„гҒЁд»®е®ҡгҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-45-2пјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Costs to Fulfill - Interaction with Other Standards
questions.stem: Quarton Co. incurs various costs to fulfill a long-term customer contract and is evaluating whether to capitalize them under the contract-cost guidance of ASC 340-40. Which of the following correctly describes the first step in that evaluation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine whether the costs are within the scope of other authoritative guidance, such as inventory, property, plant and equipment, or internal-use software; ASC 340-40 applies only if they are not

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize all fulfillment costs as long as the contract is expected to be profitable

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense all fulfillment costs as incurred unless the customer reimburses them separately

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compare the costs to the contract's transaction price and capitalize any costs below that amount

questions.explanation: ASC 340-40-25-5 applies only to costs incurred in fulfilling a contract that are not within the scope of another topic. The entity must first determine whether the costs are governed by other guidance вҖ” for example, ASC 330 (inventory), ASC 360 (property, plant, and equipment), or ASC 350-40 (internal-use software) вҖ” and apply that guidance if so. Only costs outside those topics are evaluated under the three ASC 340-40 capitalization criteria (directly related to a contract, generate or enhance resources used to satisfy future performance, and expected to be recovered). Contract profitability alone does not justify capitalization, expensing everything ignores the capitalization requirement when the criteria are met, and comparing costs to the transaction price is not a recognition test.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 340-40-25-5 гҒҜгҖҒд»–гҒ®гғҲгғ”гғғгӮҜгҒ®йҒ©з”ЁзҜ„еӣІгҒ«еҗ«гҒҫгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„еҘ‘зҙ„еұҘиЎҢгӮігӮ№гғҲгҒ«гҒ®гҒҝйҒ©з”ЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮгҒҫгҒҡеҪ“и©ІгӮігӮ№гғҲгҒҢд»–гҒ®еҹәжә–пјҲASC 330 жЈҡеҚёиіҮз”ЈгҖҒASC 360 жңүеҪўеӣәе®ҡиіҮз”ЈгҖҒASC 350-40 иҮӘзӨҫеҲ©з”ЁгӮҪгғ•гғҲгӮҰгӮ§гӮўзӯүпјүгҒ®йҒ©з”ЁзҜ„еӣІгҒӢгӮ’еҲӨе®ҡгҒ—гҖҒи©ІеҪ“гҒҷгӮҢгҒ°гҒқгҒ®еҹәжә–гӮ’йҒ©з”ЁгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮд»–еҹәжә–гҒ®зҜ„еӣІеӨ–гҒ®гӮігӮ№гғҲгҒ®гҒҝгҖҒASC 340-40 гҒ® 3 иҰҒд»¶пјҲеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ«зӣҙжҺҘй–ўйҖЈгҖҒе°ҶжқҘгҒ®еұҘиЎҢгҒ«дҪҝз”ЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢиіҮжәҗгӮ’еүөеҮәгғ»еў—еј·гҖҒеӣһеҸҺеҸҜиғҪиҰӢиҫјгҒҝпјүгҒ§иіҮз”ЈеҢ–гӮ’еҲӨе®ҡгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®еҸҺзӣҠжҖ§гҒ гҒ‘гҒ§гҒҜиіҮз”ЈеҢ–гҒ®ж №жӢ гҒ«гҒӘгӮүгҒҡгҖҒеҸ–еј•дҫЎж јгҒЁгҒ®жҜ”ијғгӮӮиӘҚиӯҳеҹәжә–гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Practical Expedient - Expensing Costs to Obtain
questions.stem: Trellis Co. pays a $5,000 sales commission to obtain a customer contract with a term of nine months. There are no anticipated renewals. Under ASC 340-40, how may Trellis account for the commission?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize the commission and amortize it over the entity's average customer life of five years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expense the commission when incurred, using the practical expedient for costs with an amortization period of one year or less

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize the commission and amortize it only when the contract becomes profitable

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer the commission until the customer makes its first payment

questions.explanation: ASC 340-40-25-4 provides a practical expedient allowing an entity to expense incremental costs of obtaining a contract when incurred if the amortization period of the asset that would otherwise be recognized is one year or less. Because the contract term is nine months with no anticipated renewals, the amortization period does not exceed one year and the commission may be expensed immediately. Amortizing over a five-year average customer life contradicts the facts, which specify no anticipated renewals. Conditioning amortization on profitability or deferring recognition until the first customer payment are not recognition models in the standard.

questions.stem: Donovan Co. completes production of goods ordered by a customer who requests, for a substantive business reason, that Donovan retain physical possession until the customer's new warehouse is ready. The goods are complete and ready for transfer, and Donovan cannot use or redirect them under the contract. However, the goods are stored commingled with Donovan's general inventory and have not been segregated or otherwise identified as belonging to the customer. May Donovan recognize revenue under the bill-and-hold guidance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the customer requested the arrangement for a substantive reason

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the goods are complete and ready for physical transfer

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the goods have not been identified separately as belonging to the customer, so control has not transferred

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because revenue can never be recognized while the seller retains physical possession

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-55-83 requires all four bill-and-hold criteria to be met: (1) a substantive reason for the arrangement, (2) the product is identified separately as belonging to the customer, (3) the product is currently ready for physical transfer, and (4) the entity cannot use the product or direct it to another customer. Donovan meets the substantive-reason, readiness, and no-redirection criteria, but the goods remain commingled with general inventory and are not separately identified, so the customer has not obtained control and revenue cannot be recognized. A substantive customer request or product readiness alone does not satisfy all four criteria. The categorical statement that possession always precludes revenue is wrong вҖ” that is precisely what the bill-and-hold guidance permits when all criteria are met.

questions.stem: Aldergate Co. delivers 500 units of product (cost $40 per unit) to Westmart, a retailer, on consignment. Westmart is obligated to remit the selling price of $90 per unit less a 10% commission for each unit sold, and may return unsold units at any time. During the period, Westmart sells 320 units to end customers. What amount of revenue should Aldergate recognize for the period?
questions.explanation: In a consignment arrangement, the consignee does not obtain control of the goods, so the consignor recognizes revenue only when the goods are sold to end customers (ASC 606-10-55-80). Aldergate's revenue is the gross selling price of the units sold: 320 x $90 = $28,800; the 10% commission of $2,880 is recognized as commission expense, not netted against revenue, because Aldergate is the principal in the sale to end customers. $45,000 (500 x $90) treats the consignment delivery itself as a sale of all units. $25,920 improperly nets the commission against revenue. $12,800 (320 x $40) is the cost of goods sold for the units sold, not revenue.

questions.subtopic: Repurchase Agreement Accounted for as a Financing
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Ridgemont Co. transfers equipment to a customer for $300,000 cash and simultaneously agrees to repurchase the equipment on December 31, Year 1 for $330,000, an amount greater than the original selling price. How should Ridgemont account for this arrangement in Year 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize no revenue; record a $300,000 financial liability and recognize $30,000 of interest expense over the year, keeping the equipment on its books

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize $300,000 of revenue on January 1 and record the repurchase as a new inventory acquisition on December 31

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize $330,000 of revenue on January 1

choices.label: D
choices.text: Account for the arrangement as an operating lease of the equipment to the customer

questions.explanation: A forward obligating the entity to repurchase the asset at a price equal to or greater than the original selling price is accounted for as a financing arrangement (ASC 606-10-55-68 through 55-70): the entity continues to recognize the asset, records the cash received as a financial liability, and recognizes the excess of the repurchase price over the original price вҖ” $330,000 - $300,000 = $30,000 вҖ” as interest expense over the period. The customer never obtains control, so no revenue arises. Recognizing $300,000 or $330,000 of revenue treats the transfer as a sale despite the repurchase obligation. Lease treatment applies only when the repurchase price is less than the original selling price (for a forward or call option), not when it is greater.
questions.explanationJa: еҪ“еҲқиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јд»ҘдёҠгҒ®дҫЎж јгҒ§иІ·гҒ„жҲ»гҒҷе…ҲжёЎеҘ‘зҙ„пјҲforwardпјүгҒҢд»ҳгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹе ҙеҗҲгҖҒйҮ‘иһҚеҸ–еј•пјҲfinancingпјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢпјҲASC 606-10-55-68гҖң55-70пјүпјҡиіҮз”ЈгҒ®иӘҚиӯҳгӮ’з¶ҷз¶ҡгҒ—гҖҒеҸ—й ҳзҸҫйҮ‘ $300,000 гӮ’йҮ‘иһҚиІ еӮөгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰиЁҲдёҠгҖҒиІ·жҲ»дҫЎж јгҒЁиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгҒ®е·®йЎҚ $330,000вҲ’$300,000пјқ$30,000 гӮ’жңҹй–“гҒ«гӮҸгҒҹгӮҠж”Ҝжү•еҲ©жҒҜгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰиӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮйЎ§е®ўгҒҜж”Ҝй…ҚгӮ’зҚІеҫ—гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒеҸҺзӣҠгҒҜз”ҹгҒҳгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ$300,000 гӮ„ $330,000 гҒ®еҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳгҒҜиІ·жҲ»зҫ©еӢҷгӮ’з„ЎиҰ–гҒ—гҒҹеЈІеҚҙеҮҰзҗҶгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒгғӘгғјгӮ№еҮҰзҗҶгҒҜиІ·жҲ»дҫЎж јгҒҢеҪ“еҲқиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгӮ’дёӢеӣһгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҒ®еҸ–жүұгҒ„гҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Nonrefundable Upfront Fee - Expected Period of Benefit
questions.stem: Streamcast Co. charges new subscribers a nonrefundable activation fee of $120 plus a monthly service fee of $30. The activation activities do not transfer a good or service to the customer. Customers subscribe month-to-month, but the renewal option provides a material right, and the average expected customer relationship is 24 months. What total revenue should Streamcast recognize for a new subscriber's first month of service?
questions.explanation: Because the activation activities do not transfer a distinct good or service, the nonrefundable upfront fee is an advance payment for future services and is recognized over the period the customer is expected to benefit вҖ” here, the 24-month expected relationship (ASC 606-10-55-51). Monthly recognition of the fee is $120 / 24 = $5, so first-month revenue is $30 + $5 = $35. $30 omits the activation-fee amortization entirely. $40 amortizes the fee over only 12 months. $150 recognizes the entire activation fee immediately, which is improper because the fee does not relate to a transferred good or service.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, an entity is generally required to disclose the aggregate amount of the transaction price allocated to remaining performance obligations. For which of the following contracts is the entity exempt from this disclosure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A three-year fixed-price construction contract that is 90% complete

choices.label: B
choices.text: A five-year software maintenance contract with annual fixed fees

choices.label: C
choices.text: A contract with an original expected duration of one year or less

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any contract for which the entity has recognized a contract liability

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-50-14 exempts an entity from disclosing remaining performance obligations for contracts with an original expected duration of one year or less, and for contracts where revenue is recognized under the right-to-invoice practical expedient. A multi-year construction contract remains subject to the disclosure even when nearly complete, because the exemption is based on the contract's original expected duration, not the remaining work. A five-year maintenance contract clearly exceeds one year. The existence of a contract liability has no bearing on the exemption; contract liabilities have their own separate disclosure requirements.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-50-14 гҒҜгҖҒеҪ“еҲқгҒ®дәҲжғіеҘ‘зҙ„жңҹй–“гҒҢ 1 е№ҙд»ҘеҶ…гҒ®еҘ‘зҙ„гҖҒгҒҠгӮҲгҒі right-to-invoice гҒ®е®ҹеӢҷдёҠгҒ®дҫҝжі•гӮ’йҒ©з”ЁгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ«гҒӨгҒ„гҒҰгҖҒж®ӢеӯҳеұҘиЎҢзҫ©еӢҷгҒ®й–ӢзӨәгӮ’е…ҚйҷӨгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮ3 е№ҙгҒ®е·ҘдәӢеҘ‘зҙ„гҒҜ 90% е®ҢжҲҗгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒҰгӮӮе…ҚйҷӨгҒ•гӮҢгҒӘгҒ„ вҖ” е…ҚйҷӨгҒҜж®ӢдҪңжҘӯгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҢеҪ“еҲқгҒ®дәҲжғіжңҹй–“гҖҚгҒ§еҲӨе®ҡгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ5 е№ҙгҒ®дҝқе®ҲеҘ‘зҙ„гҒҜжҳҺгӮүгҒӢгҒ« 1 е№ҙгӮ’и¶…гҒҲгӮӢгҖӮеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒ®жңүз„ЎгҒҜе…ҚйҷӨгҒЁз„Ўй–ўдҝӮгҒ§гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„иІ еӮөгҒ«гҒҜеҲҘйҖ”гҒ®й–ӢзӨәиҰҒжұӮгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Allocating Variable Consideration Within a Series
questions.stem: Velocity Co. provides monthly data-processing services under a two-year contract accounted for as a series of distinct service periods constituting a single performance obligation. The fee for each month varies based on the volume of transactions processed that month, and the variable fee reflects the value delivered to the customer in that month. How should Velocity recognize the variable monthly fees?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Estimate total fees for the two years at inception and recognize them on a straight-line basis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defer all variable fees until the end of the contract when total volume is known

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize each month's fee evenly over the remaining contract term

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allocate each month's variable fee entirely to the distinct monthly service period in which it is earned, because the fee relates specifically to that period's service and the allocation is consistent with the allocation objective

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-32-39 and 32-40 permit allocating variable consideration entirely to a distinct good or service in a series when (1) the variable payment relates specifically to the entity's efforts to transfer that distinct service, and (2) the allocation is consistent with the allocation objective. Because each month's fee varies with that month's processing volume and reflects the value delivered that month, both conditions are met and each month's fee is recognized in that month. Straight-lining an inception estimate would smooth revenue in a way that does not depict the value transferred each period. Deferring until contract end ignores satisfied performance. Spreading each month's fee over the remaining term has no basis in the allocation guidance.

questions.stem: Brightway Retail collects sales taxes from customers on behalf of state and local governments. Under ASC 606, which of the following correctly describes the accounting policy election available for these taxes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity may elect to exclude from the transaction price all sales taxes collected from customers that are imposed on and concurrent with specific revenue-producing transactions

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity must always include sales taxes in revenue and report the remittance as an expense

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sales taxes are included in revenue only if the entity is the principal in the underlying sale

choices.label: D
choices.text: The entity may exclude sales taxes from revenue only if the amounts are immaterial

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-32-2A provides an accounting policy election to exclude from the measurement of the transaction price all taxes assessed by a governmental authority that are imposed on and concurrent with a specific revenue-producing transaction and collected from customers (such as sales, use, and some excise taxes). An entity making this election presents revenue net of such taxes for all affected transactions. Mandatory gross presentation with remittance expense is not required вҖ” that is one permissible outcome of a transaction-by-transaction principal analysis if the election is not made. Principal status in the sale of goods does not determine the treatment of taxes when the election is available, and materiality is not the basis for the election.

questions.stem: Techguard Co. sells a one-year contract that obligates it to provide unlimited telephone and online technical support whenever the customer requests it. The customer benefits from Techguard standing ready throughout the year, and historical usage does not indicate any seasonal or other identifiable pattern of benefit. How should Techguard recognize the contract revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize revenue each time a support call is answered, in proportion to calls received

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize revenue ratably over the one-year contract period, because the obligation is to stand ready and the customer benefits evenly over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize all revenue at contract inception when the support line is activated

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer all revenue until the contract expires and total usage is known

questions.explanation: A stand-ready obligation is a promise to make a service available, and the customer benefits from the assurance of availability throughout the period regardless of actual usage. When no identifiable pattern of benefit exists, a time-based (ratable) measure of progress is appropriate over the one-year term. Recognizing revenue per call mismeasures performance because the obligation is availability, not a specified number of calls вҖ” a customer who never calls still received what was promised. Full recognition at inception ignores the year-long service period. Deferral until expiration contradicts over-time satisfaction of the obligation.

questions.stem: Metroline Co. sells prepaid fare cards that do not expire. For a new card program, Metroline has no historical data and cannot conclude that it expects to be entitled to any breakage amount. Under ASC 606, when should Metroline recognize revenue for the amounts that customers ultimately never redeem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the date the cards are sold, in proportion to an industry-average breakage rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ratably over the average period during which cards are typically used

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the likelihood of the customer exercising its remaining rights becomes remote

choices.label: D
choices.text: Never, because the cards do not expire

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-55-48 distinguishes two cases: if an entity expects to be entitled to a breakage amount, it recognizes that amount in proportion to the pattern of rights exercised by the customer; if it does not expect to be entitled to breakage, it recognizes the unredeemed amounts only when the likelihood of the customer exercising its remaining rights becomes remote. Because Metroline lacks the history to support an expected breakage amount, the remote threshold applies. Using an industry-average rate at the sale date contradicts the inability to support an entitlement expectation and would also be subject to the constraint. Ratable recognition over an average usage period is not the prescribed model. Permanent deferral is wrong вҖ” even non-expiring obligations are derecognized when exercise becomes remote (subject to unclaimed-property laws).
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-55-48 гҒҜ 2 гҒӨгҒ®е ҙеҗҲгӮ’еҢәеҲҘгҒҷгӮӢпјҡйқһиЎҢдҪҝйғЁеҲҶпјҲbreakageпјүгҒёгҒ®жЁ©еҲ©гӮ’еҫ—гӮӢгҒЁиҰӢиҫјгӮҖе ҙеҗҲгҒҜйЎ§е®ўгҒ®жЁ©еҲ©иЎҢдҪҝгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒ«жҜ”дҫӢгҒ—гҒҰиӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҖҒиҰӢиҫјгҒҫгҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгҒҜйЎ§е®ўгҒҢж®ӢеӯҳжЁ©еҲ©гӮ’иЎҢдҪҝгҒҷгӮӢеҸҜиғҪжҖ§гҒҢжҘөгӮҒгҒҰдҪҺгҒҸпјҲremoteпјүгҒӘгҒЈгҒҹжҷӮзӮ№гҒ§иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮMetroline гҒ«гҒҜжңҹеҫ… breakage гӮ’иЈҸд»ҳгҒ‘гӮӢе®ҹзёҫгҒҢгҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒ remote еҹәжә–гӮ’йҒ©з”ЁгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮиІ©еЈІжҷӮгҒ«жҘӯз•Ңе№іеқҮзҺҮгҒ§иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜжЁ©еҲ©гҒёгҒ®жңҹеҫ…гӮ’иЈҸд»ҳгҒ‘гӮүгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶеүҚжҸҗгҒЁзҹӣзӣҫгҒ—гҖҒе№іеқҮеҲ©з”Ёжңҹй–“гҒ§гҒ®жҢүеҲҶиӘҚиӯҳгӮӮеҹәжә–гҒ®е®ҡгӮҒгӮӢж–№жі•гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮз„ЎжңҹйҷҗгӮ«гғјгғүгҒ§гӮӮиЎҢдҪҝеҸҜиғҪжҖ§гҒҢ remote гҒ«гҒӘгӮҢгҒ°иӘҚиӯҳгҒҷгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҢиӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖҚгӮӮиӘӨгӮҠпјҲжңӘи«ӢжұӮиІЎз”Јжі•гҖ”escheatгҖ•гҒ®еҜҫиұЎйғЁеҲҶгӮ’йҷӨгҒҸпјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Combined Contracts - Single Measure of Progress
questions.stem: Fairfield Co. enters into two contracts with the same customer at the same time, negotiated as a package with a single commercial objective: Contract 1 for $80,000 and Contract 2 for $40,000. The contracts are combined under ASC 606 and together contain a single performance obligation satisfied over time, measured using the cost-to-cost method. By year-end, Fairfield has incurred $45,000 of costs against total estimated costs of $90,000. What revenue should Fairfield recognize for the year?
questions.explanation: Because the contracts were negotiated as a package with a single commercial objective, they are combined and accounted for as a single contract with a combined transaction price of $80,000 + $40,000 = $120,000 (ASC 606-10-25-9). Progress under the cost-to-cost method is $45,000 / $90,000 = 50%, so revenue is $120,000 x 50% = $60,000. $80,000 treats Contract 1 as fully earned on a standalone basis, ignoring the combination. $45,000 equals the costs incurred, not the revenue. $40,000 is the Contract 2 price, which has no significance once the contracts are combined.

questions.subtopic: Five-Step Model - Placement of the Constraint
questions.stem: Under the five-step revenue recognition model of ASC 606, applying the constraint on variable consideration (limiting estimates to the amount for which a significant revenue reversal is not probable) is performed as part of which step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Step 1 - Identify the contract with the customer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Step 2 - Identify the performance obligations in the contract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Step 3 - Determine the transaction price

choices.label: D
choices.text: Step 4 - Allocate the transaction price to the performance obligations

questions.explanation: The constraint on variable consideration is part of determining the transaction price. When the consideration includes a variable amount, the entity estimates it using the expected value or most likely amount method, and then includes in the transaction price only the amount for which it is probable that a significant reversal of cumulative revenue will not occur when the uncertainty is resolved. Identifying the contract addresses enforceability and collectibility, identifying performance obligations addresses distinct goods or services, and allocation deals with assigning an already-determined transaction price based on relative standalone selling prices.

questions.subtopic: Variable Consideration - Selecting the Estimation Method
questions.stem: Under ASC 606, an entity must estimate variable consideration using either the expected value method or the most likely amount method. The most likely amount method is generally the more appropriate method when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The contract has only two possible outcomes, such as a performance bonus that is either fully earned or not earned at all

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity has a large number of contracts with similar characteristics

choices.label: C
choices.text: The variable consideration can take on a wide range of possible amounts

choices.label: D
choices.text: The entity wishes to minimize the transaction price to avoid revenue reversals

questions.explanation: ASC 606 requires the entity to use the method that better predicts the amount of consideration to which it will be entitled. The most likely amount method is appropriate when the contract has only a limited number of possible outcomes вҖ” typically a binary outcome such as a bonus that is either earned in full or not earned. The expected value (probability-weighted) method better predicts the outcome when the entity has a large number of contracts with similar characteristics or when there is a wide range of possible outcomes. The choice of method is not an accounting policy election made to manage the size of the transaction price; minimizing revenue is achieved through the separate constraint guidance, not through method selection.

questions.subtopic: Constraint - Full Constraint When No Predictive Experience
questions.stem: Marlow Engineering enters into a contract to design a specialized production line for a fixed fee of $800,000 plus a $100,000 bonus payable only if the line passes the customer's stress test on the first attempt. Marlow has never designed this type of production line before, the outcome of the stress test depends heavily on operating conditions controlled by the customer, and the bonus is a binary all-or-nothing amount. At contract inception, what transaction price should Marlow use?
questions.explanation: Variable consideration is included in the transaction price only to the extent it is probable that a significant reversal of cumulative revenue will not occur. Factors indicating the constraint should be applied include: the amount is highly susceptible to factors outside the entity's influence (the stress test depends on conditions the customer controls), the entity has limited experience with similar contracts (it has never designed this type of line), and the contract has a large number or broad range of possible outcomes relative to the bonus. Given these factors, the entire $100,000 bonus is constrained at inception, and the transaction price is the fixed fee of $800,000. $900,000 includes the fully constrained bonus; $850,000 and $880,000 represent unsupported partial probability weightings of a binary bonus that should be fully excluded.

questions.subtopic: Principal vs Agent - Delivery Platform Net Revenue
questions.stem: QuickPlate operates a meal-ordering platform. Restaurants list their own menus and set all menu prices, prepare the food, and are responsible for food quality and remaking unsatisfactory orders. QuickPlate never takes possession of the food and bears no inventory risk. During June, customers paid QuickPlate $60,000 for orders, and QuickPlate remitted $48,000 to the restaurants, retaining the difference as its platform fee. How much revenue should QuickPlate recognize for June?
questions.explanation: QuickPlate is an agent because it does not control the food before it is transferred to the customer: the restaurants set the prices, are primarily responsible for fulfilling the promise (preparing and remaking the food), and QuickPlate bears no inventory risk. An agent recognizes revenue equal to the fee or commission it retains for arranging the transaction: $60,000 collected less $48,000 remitted = $12,000. Recognizing the full $60,000 would be appropriate only if QuickPlate were the principal. $48,000 is the amount owed to the restaurants, not QuickPlate's consideration, and recognizing nothing ignores the commission earned for the arranging service.

questions.stem: Halverson Co. has a contract to provide 36 monthly inspections (each month is distinct) for $3,600. After 12 inspections, the customer negotiates a modification that cancels 12 of the remaining 24 inspections and reduces the remaining contract price to a level that does not reflect the standalone selling price of the remaining services. Under ASC 606, how should Halverson account for this modification?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a termination of the existing contract and creation of a new contract, recognizing the remaining consideration over the remaining inspections prospectively

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a separate contract, leaving the accounting for the original 36-inspection contract unchanged

choices.label: C
choices.text: With a cumulative catch-up adjustment to revenue recognized for the 12 completed inspections

choices.label: D
choices.text: By deferring all remaining consideration until the modified contract is completed

questions.explanation: When a modification is not a separate contract and the remaining goods or services are distinct from those already transferred, the modification is accounted for as a termination of the existing contract and the creation of a new contract. The consideration not yet recognized (plus or minus any price change) is allocated to the remaining performance obligations and recognized prospectively. A modification qualifies as a separate contract only when it adds distinct goods or services at their standalone selling price вҖ” a reduction in scope can never meet that test. A cumulative catch-up applies only when the remaining goods or services are not distinct (a single partially satisfied performance obligation), which is not the case here since each monthly inspection is distinct. Deferring everything until completion has no basis in ASC 606.

questions.subtopic: Functional IP - Exception Requiring Over-Time Recognition
questions.stem: A license of functional intellectual property is generally recognized at a point in time. Under ASC 606, revenue from a functional IP license is instead recognized over time when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The license term exceeds one year and the customer pays in annual installments

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entity continues to market and advertise the underlying intellectual property during the license period

choices.label: C
choices.text: The functionality of the IP is expected to change substantively as a result of the entity's ongoing activities, and the customer is contractually or practically required to use the updated IP

choices.label: D
choices.text: The license is bundled with a distinct installation service in the same contract

questions.explanation: Functional IP (software, drug formulas, completed media content) derives its utility from standalone functionality, so the customer obtains substantially all the benefit when the license is transferred вҖ” point-in-time recognition. The exception arises when both of two criteria are met: the functionality is expected to change substantively because of activities of the entity that are not a separate performance obligation, and the customer is contractually or practically required to use the updated IP. In that case the customer's benefit depends on ongoing access, and revenue is recognized over time. A multi-year term with installment payments affects only payment timing, marketing activities are relevant to symbolic (not functional) IP, and bundling with a distinct service simply creates two separate performance obligations without changing the license's pattern of recognition.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, which of the following licensed items is most likely to be classified as functional intellectual property, resulting in point-in-time revenue recognition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A consumer brand name licensed to an apparel maker

choices.label: B
choices.text: A completed motion picture licensed to a television network

choices.label: C
choices.text: A professional sports team's logo licensed to a merchandise vendor

choices.label: D
choices.text: A franchise trade name licensed to a restaurant operator

questions.explanation: Functional IP has significant standalone functionality вҖ” the customer can derive substantial benefit from the IP as it exists when transferred, without ongoing support from the licensor. A completed film can be broadcast and generate value on its own, so it is functional IP recognized at the point in time the network can begin to use it. Brand names, team logos, and franchise trade names are symbolic IP: their value derives from the licensor's past or ongoing activities (maintaining the brand, fielding the team, operating the franchise system), so the related revenue is recognized over the license term.

questions.subtopic: Costs to Fulfill - Amounts Eligible for Capitalization
questions.stem: Bryton Services signs a 5-year data-management contract and incurs the following costs before beginning to provide services: direct labor to design the customer's data-migration plan $30,000; materials used to build customer-specific interfaces $20,000; depreciation of equipment allocated to the contract setup $10,000; general and administrative overhead not chargeable to the customer $15,000; and wages for labor hours wasted due to an internal scheduling error $5,000. The setup costs relate directly to the contract, generate resources that will be used to satisfy the obligation, and are expected to be recovered. What amount should Bryton capitalize as costs to fulfill the contract?
questions.explanation: Costs to fulfill a contract are capitalized when they relate directly to the contract, enhance resources that will be used to satisfy future performance obligations, and are expected to be recovered. Direct labor ($30,000), direct materials ($20,000), and allocated costs that relate directly to the contract such as equipment depreciation ($10,000) qualify: $30,000 + $20,000 + $10,000 = $60,000. General and administrative costs not explicitly chargeable to the customer ($15,000) and costs of wasted labor ($5,000) must be expensed as incurred. $80,000 incorrectly capitalizes everything; $50,000 incorrectly excludes the allocable depreciation, which ASC 340-40 explicitly permits; $65,000 incorrectly includes the wasted labor.

questions.subtopic: Costs to Obtain - Amortization with Commensurate Renewal Commission
questions.stem: Calloway Co. pays a salesperson a $36,000 commission for obtaining a 3-year customer contract. If the customer renews, Calloway will pay a renewal commission of the same rate that is commensurate with the initial commission. Customers of this type typically remain with Calloway for 6 years including renewals. What commission expense (amortization) should Calloway recognize in Year 1?
questions.explanation: A capitalized cost of obtaining a contract is amortized over the period of the goods or services to which the asset relates, which includes anticipated renewal periods only when the renewal commission is not commensurate with the initial commission. Here the renewal commission is commensurate (paid at the same rate for the renewal), so the $36,000 asset relates only to the initial 3-year term: $36,000 / 3 years = $12,000 per year. Amortizing over 6 years ($6,000 per year) would be correct only if no commensurate renewal commission were paid. Expensing the full $36,000 immediately is allowed under the practical expedient only when the amortization period would be one year or less. $18,000 reflects an unsupported 2-year period.

questions.stem: Orton Manufacturing completes production of goods ordered by a customer on December 28, Year 1. Because Orton's shipping department is backlogged, Orton decides to hold the completed goods in its warehouse until mid-January. The goods are separately identified, ready for physical transfer, and will not be used to fill other orders. The customer did not request the delay. May Orton recognize revenue in Year 1 under a bill-and-hold arrangement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the goods are complete, separately identified, and ready for transfer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because title passes when production is complete under the contract

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the reason for the bill-and-hold arrangement must be substantive and requested by the customer, and here the delay originates with the seller

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because revenue on manufactured goods can never be recognized before shipment under any circumstances

questions.explanation: For revenue to be recognized in a bill-and-hold arrangement, all of the following must be met: the reason for the arrangement must be substantive (for example, the customer requested it because it lacks storage space), the product must be separately identified as the customer's, it must be ready for physical transfer, and the entity cannot have the ability to use it or direct it to another customer. Here the delay arises from the seller's own shipping backlog, not from a substantive customer request, so control has not transferred and revenue cannot be recognized in Year 1. The statement that goods are complete and identified addresses only some of the criteria; passage of title alone does not establish transfer of control; and the claim that revenue can never be recognized before shipment is wrong because a qualifying bill-and-hold sale does permit pre-shipment recognition.

questions.subtopic: Repurchase Agreements - Put Option Without Significant Incentive
questions.stem: Davenport Co. sells specialized equipment to a customer for $400,000 and grants the customer a put option to sell the equipment back to Davenport for $250,000 at the end of two years. The expected market value of the equipment at the end of two years is $300,000, and Davenport concludes the customer does not have a significant economic incentive to exercise the option. How should Davenport account for the transaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a lease, with the $400,000 received treated as lease payments

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a sale of a product with a right of return

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a financing arrangement, with a $400,000 liability and continued recognition of the equipment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer all revenue until the put option expires unexercised

questions.explanation: When a customer holds a put option, the accounting depends on whether the customer has a significant economic incentive to exercise it. The repurchase price of $250,000 is below the expected market value of $300,000, so a rational customer would sell in the market rather than exercise the put, and Davenport concluded there is no significant incentive. With no significant economic incentive, the transaction is accounted for as a sale with a right of return: recognize revenue, a refund liability for expected exercises, and an asset for the right to recover the equipment as applicable. Lease accounting would apply only if the customer had a significant economic incentive to exercise (repurchase price below original price with incentive); financing treatment applies when the repurchase price is equal to or greater than the original selling price; and full deferral until expiration is not the ASC 606 model for put options lacking a significant incentive.

questions.subtopic: Service-Type Warranty - Allocation of Transaction Price
questions.stem: Trenholm Co. sells industrial pumps for a single contract price of $45,000 each, which includes a 4-year maintenance plan that goes well beyond assuring the pump complies with agreed specifications. The standalone selling price of the pump is $40,000 and the standalone selling price of the 4-year maintenance plan is $10,000. How much revenue should Trenholm recognize for the pump upon delivery?
questions.explanation: The maintenance plan provides a service beyond assurance that the product meets agreed specifications, so it is a service-type warranty вҖ” a separate performance obligation. The $45,000 transaction price is allocated based on relative standalone selling prices: pump = $45,000 Г— ($40,000 / $50,000) = $36,000; maintenance = $45,000 Г— ($10,000 / $50,000) = $9,000, recognized over the 4-year service period. Recognizing $45,000 at delivery ignores the separate service obligation; $40,000 uses the pump's standalone selling price without allocating the bundle discount proportionately; and $9,000 is the amount allocated to the maintenance plan, not the pump.

questions.subtopic: Loyalty Program - Allocation and Redemption-Based Recognition
questions.stem: Kestrel Stores launches a loyalty program. During Year 1, customers purchase goods for $90,000 and earn points with an estimated standalone selling price of $10,000 (after considering the likelihood of redemption). By the end of Year 1, customers have redeemed 40% of the points expected to be redeemed, and the redemption estimate is unchanged. What total revenue should Kestrel recognize for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Loyalty points provide the customer a material right and are a separate performance obligation. The $90,000 is allocated on a relative standalone selling price basis: goods = $90,000 Г— ($90,000 / $100,000) = $81,000, recognized at sale; points = $90,000 Г— ($10,000 / $100,000) = $9,000, deferred as a contract liability. Revenue on the points is recognized as they are redeemed in proportion to total expected redemptions: $9,000 Г— 40% = $3,600. Total Year 1 revenue = $81,000 + $3,600 = $84,600. $90,000 ignores the deferral of the material right; $81,000 omits the revenue from redeemed points; $86,400 incorrectly applies the 40% redemption rate to a 60% remaining-deferral computation ($81,000 + $5,400).

questions.subtopic: Exchanges for Same-Type Products Not Treated as Returns
questions.stem: Lindale Apparel sells clothing and allows customers to exchange items for the same product in a different size or color of the same quality and price. Customers may not receive cash refunds. Under ASC 606, how should Lindale account for these exchange rights at the time of sale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Record a refund liability and a return asset for expected exchanges

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize revenue in full at the time of sale; exchanges of one product for another of the same type, quality, condition, and price are not considered returns

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defer all revenue until the exchange period lapses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduce revenue by the estimated cost of processing expected exchanges

questions.explanation: ASC 606 specifically states that exchanges by customers of one product for another of the same type, quality, condition, and price (for example, one color or size for another) are not considered returns for accounting purposes. Because the customer cannot obtain a refund and receives an equivalent product, the entity's revenue is unaffected, so revenue is recognized in full at sale. A refund liability and return asset are required only for genuine rights of return where consideration may be refunded or credited. Deferring revenue until the exchange window closes overstates the obligation, and processing costs of exchanges are operating expenses, not a reduction of revenue.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Pemberton Co. delivers machinery to a customer in exchange for a single payment of $266,200 due on December 31, Year 3. The contract contains a significant financing component, and the rate that would be reflected in a separate financing transaction between Pemberton and the customer is 10%. ($266,200 / 1.10^3 = $200,000.) What amount of revenue from the sale of the machinery should Pemberton recognize on January 1, Year 1?
questions.explanation: When a contract contains a significant financing component, the transaction price is adjusted to reflect the cash selling price at the time control transfers. The present value of $266,200 discounted three years at 10% is $266,200 / 1.331 = $200,000, which is the revenue recognized on delivery. The $66,200 difference is recognized as interest income over the three years using the effective interest method ($20,000 in Year 1, $22,000 in Year 2, $24,200 in Year 3). $266,200 fails to separate the financing component; $220,000 and $242,000 are the receivable balances after one and two years of interest accretion, not the date-of-sale revenue.

questions.subtopic: Over Time - Reperformance Test for Simultaneous Consumption
questions.stem: Coastal Freight Co. is transporting a customer's goods from Chicago to Seattle under a single freight contract. At year-end, the shipment is halfway to its destination. In evaluating whether the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits of Coastal's performance, which analysis supports recognizing the freight revenue over time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If another carrier took over at the midpoint, it would not need to re-transport the goods from Chicago, demonstrating that the customer has already consumed the benefit of the work performed to date

choices.label: B
choices.text: The customer pays the freight charge only upon delivery at the final destination, so the benefit is received over the transit period

choices.label: C
choices.text: The goods belong to the customer throughout transit, so any service performed on them automatically qualifies for over-time recognition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Freight services are short in duration, and ASC 606 permits over-time recognition for all contracts shorter than one year

questions.explanation: When it is difficult to assess directly whether a customer simultaneously receives and consumes benefits, ASC 606 provides a reperformance test: if another entity stepping in to complete the remaining obligation would not need to substantially re-perform the work completed to date, the customer has been consuming the benefit as the entity performs. A replacement carrier picking up the goods at the midpoint would carry them only the remaining distance вҖ” the first half of the haul would not be redone вҖ” so over-time recognition is supported. Payment timing does not determine the recognition pattern; customer ownership of the goods relates to a different over-time criterion (the entity's performance creates or enhances an asset the customer controls) and is not automatic; and there is no blanket over-time rule for contracts under one year.

questions.subtopic: Gift Card Breakage - Total Revenue Including Breakage
questions.stem: Avondale Retail sells $300,000 of gift cards during Year 1. Based on extensive historical experience, Avondale expects 10% of the card value ($30,000) will never be redeemed, and it is entitled to keep unredeemed amounts. By the end of Year 1, customers have redeemed $135,000 of gift cards. Using the proportional (expected-redemption) method, what total revenue should Avondale recognize from the gift card program in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Expected redemptions total $300,000 вҲ’ $30,000 = $270,000. Actual redemptions of $135,000 represent 50% of expected redemptions ($135,000 / $270,000). Breakage is recognized in proportion to the pattern of redemption: $30,000 Г— 50% = $15,000. Total Year 1 revenue = $135,000 (redemptions) + $15,000 (breakage) = $150,000. $135,000 ignores breakage entirely; $165,000 incorrectly recognizes all $30,000 of breakage immediately; $148,500 results from applying the 10% breakage rate to the $135,000 redeemed ($13,500) instead of using the proportion of expected redemptions.

questions.subtopic: Noncash Consideration - Subsequent Fair Value Changes
questions.stem: Whitfield Consulting agrees to provide services to a customer over six months in exchange for 2,000 shares of the customer's stock. At contract inception, the shares have a fair value of $50,000. When Whitfield completes the services and receives the shares, their fair value has risen to $58,000. Under ASC 606 (as amended), how is the $8,000 increase in fair value reflected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As additional service revenue when the shares are received

choices.label: B
choices.text: As an increase to the transaction price allocated retrospectively over the service period

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a contract asset until the shares are sold

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is excluded from revenue; revenue is measured at the $50,000 inception-date fair value, and the subsequent change is accounted for as a gain on the shares, not revenue

questions.explanation: Noncash consideration is measured at fair value at contract inception. Changes in the fair value of noncash consideration after inception that are due to the form of the consideration (such as changes in a share price) are not included in the transaction price. Revenue is therefore $50,000, and the $8,000 appreciation is accounted for under the relevant financial instruments guidance as a gain on the equity interest. Treating the increase as additional revenue or as a retrospective transaction price adjustment would improperly let market movements in the consideration's form flow through revenue, and a contract asset does not capture post-inception fair value changes of noncash consideration.

questions.subtopic: Royalty Exception - Scope Limited to Predominant IP Licenses
questions.stem: Ralston Co. enters into a contract to sell a customer specialized manufacturing equipment. The contract price includes a royalty of 2% of the customer's future product sales made using the equipment. The contract contains no license of intellectual property, or any license involved is clearly insignificant relative to the equipment. How should Ralston account for the sales-based royalty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply the sales-based royalty exception and recognize the royalty only as the customer's sales occur

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exclude the royalty from the contract entirely because it depends on the customer's actions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Treat the royalty as variable consideration: estimate it, apply the constraint, and include the resulting amount in the transaction price at inception

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize the royalty as interest income over the expected sales period

questions.explanation: The sales- or usage-based royalty exception applies only when the royalty relates solely or predominantly to a license of intellectual property. Here the royalty relates to a sale of equipment, not predominantly to an IP license, so the exception does not apply. The royalty is ordinary variable consideration: Ralston estimates it (expected value or most likely amount), applies the constraint, and includes the estimate in the transaction price at contract inception, updating it each period. Waiting until the customer's sales occur is the exception treatment available only for predominant IP licenses; ignoring the royalty understates the transaction price; and a royalty is consideration for goods, not a financing return, so interest income treatment is inapplicable.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, an entity reclassifies a contract asset to a receivable at the point when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity's right to the consideration becomes unconditional, meaning only the passage of time is required before payment is due

choices.label: B
choices.text: The customer actually pays the amount due

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity issues its annual financial statements

choices.label: D
choices.text: The remaining performance obligations in the contract are fully satisfied

questions.explanation: A contract asset is a right to consideration that is conditioned on something other than the passage of time вҖ” typically further performance. Once the right becomes unconditional (nothing remains but waiting for the due date), the entity presents a receivable. For example, a milestone billing provision may make payment conditional on completing an additional phase; when that phase is completed and the amount is billable, the contract asset becomes a receivable. Actual cash collection extinguishes a receivable rather than creating one, financial statement issuance has no effect on classification, and full satisfaction of all performance obligations is not required вҖ” a right to a progress billing can become unconditional while other obligations remain.

questions.stem: Renfrew Builders enters into a $1,200,000 fixed-price contract that qualifies for over-time recognition using the cost-to-cost input method. During Year 1, Renfrew incurs costs of $270,000 and estimates that $630,000 of additional costs will be required to complete the contract. What gross profit should Renfrew recognize in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Total estimated costs = $270,000 incurred + $630,000 remaining = $900,000. Percentage complete = $270,000 / $900,000 = 30%. Revenue recognized = $1,200,000 Г— 30% = $360,000. Gross profit = $360,000 вҲ’ $270,000 = $90,000. (Equivalently, total estimated gross profit of $1,200,000 вҲ’ $900,000 = $300,000 Г— 30% = $90,000.) $300,000 is the total expected gross profit for the entire contract, not the Year 1 portion; $360,000 is Year 1 revenue, not gross profit; $75,000 results from an incorrect percentage computed as $270,000 divided by total contract price plus other errors and has no valid basis.

questions.subtopic: Discount Allocation - Observable Evidence for Specific Obligations
questions.stem: Hartwick Co. sells three distinct products in a single contract for a bundled price of $180,000. Standalone selling prices are: Product X $40,000, Product Y $60,000, and Product Z $100,000 (total $200,000). Hartwick regularly sells Products Y and Z together as a package for $140,000, and regularly sells Product X separately for $40,000. How much of the transaction price should be allocated to Product X?
questions.explanation: A discount is allocated entirely to one or more (but not all) performance obligations when the entity has observable evidence that the entire discount relates to those specific obligations: the entity regularly sells each good on a standalone basis, regularly sells a bundle of some of those goods at a discount, and that bundle discount is substantially the same as the contract's discount. The contract discount is $200,000 вҲ’ $180,000 = $20,000, which equals the observable discount on the Y-and-Z package ($60,000 + $100,000 вҲ’ $140,000 = $20,000). Therefore the entire discount is allocated to Y and Z, and Product X is allocated its full standalone selling price of $40,000. $36,000 results from spreading the discount proportionately across all three products ($180,000 Г— 40/200), which is the default method but is overridden by the observable evidence here.

questions.subtopic: Principal vs Agent - Financial Statement Impact of Gross vs Net
questions.stem: Norcross Co. is evaluating whether it is a principal (gross presentation) or an agent (net presentation) for a product line. Which statement correctly describes the financial statement impact of the conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Net income is higher under gross presentation because revenue is larger

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gross profit in dollars is higher under gross presentation than net presentation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reported revenue and cost of sales are both higher under gross presentation, but gross profit in dollars and net income are the same under either presentation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The choice affects only the balance sheet, not the income statement

questions.explanation: If the entity is a principal, it reports the gross amount billed to the customer as revenue and the amount paid to the supplier as cost of sales.
