If it is an agent, it reports only its fee or commission as revenue. The dollar amount of gross profit and net income is identical either way вҖ” only the top line and cost line differ. The conclusion still matters because revenue size affects ratios, growth metrics, and analyst evaluation. The presentation does not change profit, so claims that net income or gross profit dollars are higher under gross presentation are wrong, and the impact is on the income statement presentation, not solely the balance sheet.

questions.stem: Under the disclosure requirements of ASC 606, which of the following must an entity disclose about its contract balances?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Revenue recognized in the reporting period that was included in the contract liability balance at the beginning of the period

choices.label: B
choices.text: The names of customers that account for the largest contract asset balances

choices.label: C
choices.text: A forecast of contract liability balances for the next five years

choices.label: D
choices.text: The interest rate implicit in each contract asset

questions.explanation: ASC 606 requires disclosure of opening and closing balances of receivables, contract assets, and contract liabilities; revenue recognized in the period that was included in the contract liability balance at the beginning of the period; and qualitative explanations of significant changes in contract balances. This shows users how deferred amounts convert into recognized revenue. Customer names relate to concentration disclosures under other guidance (and even those do not require naming customers), forecasts of future balances are not required by ASC 606, and an implicit interest rate per contract asset is not a contract-balance disclosure requirement.

questions.subtopic: Significant Financing - One-Year Practical Expedient Applied
questions.stem: On May 1, Year 1, Drummond Co. delivers custom shelving to a customer for a contract price of $80,000, with payment due on November 1, Year 1 (six months after delivery). Drummond's incremental borrowing rate is 10%, and Drummond elects all available ASC 606 practical expedients. What revenue should Drummond recognize on May 1, Year 1?
questions.explanation: ASC 606 provides a practical expedient under which an entity need not adjust the transaction price for a significant financing component if, at contract inception, it expects the period between the transfer of the good and the customer's payment to be one year or less. The gap here is six months, so the expedient applies and revenue is the full contract price of $80,000 with no interest element separated. $76,190 ($80,000 / 1.05) and $76,000 ($80,000 Г— 0.95) reflect unnecessary six-month discounting, and $72,727 ($80,000 / 1.10) applies a full year of discounting; all three improperly bifurcate a financing component the expedient permits the entity to ignore.

questions.subtopic: Output Method - Survey of Work Performed
questions.stem: Ashford Construction has a $400,000 fixed-price contract recognized over time. Ashford concludes that an output method based on independent engineering surveys of work completed best depicts the transfer of control. At year-end, the engineering survey certifies the project is 35% complete. Costs incurred to date are $360,000 and total estimated costs are $800,000. What revenue should Ashford recognize for the year?
questions.explanation: When an entity selects an output method, progress is measured by direct measurement of the value transferred to the customer вҖ” here, the independent engineering survey. Revenue = $400,000 Г— 35% = $140,000. The cost-to-cost ratio of $360,000 / $800,000 = 45%, producing $180,000, is an input measure that Ashford determined does not best depict its performance and is therefore not used. The entity applies a single method of measuring progress consistently for the performance obligation; it does not switch to whichever measure yields more revenue. $400,000 is the full contract price, appropriate only at completion, and $160,000 (40%) reflects an unsupported averaging of the two measures.

questions.stem: During Year 1, customers purchased gift cards from ABC Coffee, all of which expire on December 31 in Year 3. At the end of Year 2, some of the gift cards still have not been redeemed. How should the unredeemed gift cards be reported on ABC Coffee's year-end financial statements for Year 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As sales revenue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a current liability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a prepaid asset.

choices.label: D
choices.text: As an expense.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606, revenue is recognized when (or as) the performance obligation is satisfied. Gift cards represent deferred revenue вҖ” the company has received cash but has not yet delivered goods or services. At December 31, Year 2, the gift cards expire on December 31, Year 3, which is within one year of the balance sheet date, making the obligation a current liability. Revenue is recognized only when the gift card is redeemed or when it is considered unlikely to be redeemed (breakage, recognized proportionally). Until redemption or recognized breakage, unredeemed gift cards remain a current liability.

questions.stem: Which of the following is most likely to be considered revenue, according to the Financial Accounting Standards Board's definition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A company in the business of manufacturing scientific devices recognizes a gain from the sale of agricultural land that was held for future plant expansion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A pharmaceutical company receives cash from an insurance lawsuit settlement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A web streaming company fulfills a 12-month service term paid by customers in advance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A clothing reseller recognizes a gain from early extinguishment of long-term debt.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606 and the FASB conceptual framework, revenue is defined as inflows from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities that constitute the entity's ongoing major operations. A web streaming company fulfilling a 12-month service contract is earning revenue from its primary business activity. The gain from selling agricultural land (peripheral activity for a device manufacturer), an insurance settlement (non-operating), and a gain on debt extinguishment (financing activity) are all gains, not revenues вҖ” they arise from peripheral or incidental activities, not the entity's primary operations.

questions.stem: On September 25, Year 1, Colson Corp. sold 200,000 widgets to Cavanaugh Corp. at $5 per unit. Half of the units were delivered on November 15, Year 1, and the remaining 100,000 units were delivered on January 20, Year 2. At the time of sale, Cavanaugh paid 40% of the contract price and agreed to pay the rest in equal installments on the two delivery dates. What amount of accrual-basis revenue should Colson recognize from this sale in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under accrual-basis accounting (ASC 606), revenue is recognized when performance obligations are satisfied вҖ” i.e., when control of the goods transfers to the customer. Colson delivered 100,000 widgets on November 15, Year 1. Revenue for Year 1 = 100,000 units Г— $5 = $500,000. The remaining 100,000 units were delivered in Year 2, so $500,000 is recognized in Year 2. The cash received upfront (40% Г— $1,000,000 = $400,000) and the payment schedule do not determine when revenue is recognized under the accrual basis.

questions.stem: A shoe retailer allows customers to return shoes within 90 days of purchase. The company estimates that 5% of sales will be returned within the 90-day period. During the month, the company has sales of $200,000 and returns of sales made in prior months of $5,000. What amount should the company record as net sales revenue for new sales made during the month?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606, variable consideration such as estimated returns must be excluded from the transaction price. For the $200,000 of new sales, the estimated return rate is 5%, so estimated returns = $200,000 Г— 5% = $10,000. Net sales revenue for new sales = $200,000 вҲ’ $10,000 = $190,000. The $5,000 of actual returns from prior months relate to revenue previously recognized in prior periods and do not affect the current month's revenue from new sales.

questions.stem: During the current year, Casual Wear Co. had total retail sales of $800,000 and collected a 5% state sales tax on all sales. At the end of the prior year, Casual Wear had $4,500 in sales taxes that had not been remitted to the state authorities. During the current year, Casual Wear remitted $39,500 in state sales tax. What amount should be recorded in Casual Wear's current-year financial statements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $5,000 in sales tax payable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $39,500 in sales tax expense.

choices.label: C
choices.text: $40,000 in sales tax revenue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $840,000 in sales revenue.

questions.explanation: Sales taxes collected from customers are not revenue for the retailer вҖ” they are a liability owed to the government. Sales tax collected during the current year = $800,000 Г— 5% = $40,000. Beginning sales tax payable = $4,500. Remitted = $39,500. Ending sales tax payable = $4,500 + $40,000 вҲ’ $39,500 = $5,000. Therefore, $5,000 should be reported as sales tax payable (a current liability) at year-end.
questions.explanationJa: йЎ§е®ўгҒӢгӮүеҸҺйӣҶгҒ—гҒҹж¶ҲиІ»зЁҺгҒҜе°ҸеЈІжҘӯиҖ…гҒ®еҸҺзӣҠгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҒе·һгҒ«зҙҚд»ҳгҒҷгҒ№гҒҚиІ еӮөгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҖӮеҪ“жңҹеҸҺйӣҶж¶ҲиІ»зЁҺ = $800,000 Г— 5% = $40,000гҖӮеүҚжңҹжң«жңӘжү•ж¶ҲиІ»зЁҺ$4,500 + еҪ“жңҹеҸҺйӣҶ$40,000 вҲ’ еҪ“жңҹзҙҚд»ҳ$39,500 = $5,000гҒҢжңҹжң«жңӘжү•ж¶ҲиІ»зЁҺпјҲжөҒеӢ•иІ еӮөпјүгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰиЁҲдёҠгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Over-Time Revenue Recognition вҖ” Contract Asset vs. Liability
questions.stem: Falton Co. had the following first - year amounts related to its $9,000,000 construction contract: Actual costs incurred and paid $2,000,000 Estimated costs to complete 6,000,000 Progress billings 1,800,000 Cash collected 1,500,000 Falton Co. accounts for revenue from the contract as the performance obligation is being satisfied and measures progress on the contract based on costs incurred. What amount, if any, should Falton recognize as a current liability at year end?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-45-3, a contract liability arises only when billings exceed revenue recognized to date. Total estimated cost = $2,000,000 + $6,000,000 = $8,000,000; percentage complete = $2,000,000 / $8,000,000 = 25%. Revenue recognized = 25% Г— $9,000,000 = $2,250,000. Since billings of $1,800,000 are less than revenue recognized of $2,250,000, Falton has a contract asset (not a liability) of $450,000. Therefore, the current liability is $0.

questions.subtopic: Cost-to-Cost Method вҖ” Year-by-Year Gross Profit
questions.stem: Haft Construction Co. has consistently recognized income from long-term contracts as its construction obligations are being satisfied. On January 10, Year 1, Haft began work on a $3,000,000 construction contract to be completed in three years. The following data relate to the progress of the contract as of December 31, Year 2: Income recognized at 12/31/Year 1 $300,000 Costs incurred 1/10/Year 1 through 12/31/Year 2 1,800,000 Estimated cost to complete 600,000 If Haft measures progress toward completion of its contract obligations using the cost-to-cost approach, what amount of gross profit should Haft report in its income statement for the year ended December 31, Year 2?
questions.explanation: Total estimated cost = $1,800,000 + $600,000 = $2,400,000. Total estimated profit = $3,000,000 вҲ’ $2,400,000 = $600,000. Percentage complete through Year 2 = $1,800,000 / $2,400,000 = 75%. Cumulative profit earned through Year 2 = 75% Г— $600,000 = $450,000. Year 2 gross profit = $450,000 cumulative вҲ’ $300,000 recognized in Year 1 = $150,000 (ASC 606-10-25-27).

questions.subtopic: Distinct Performance Obligations вҖ” Point-in-Time Recognition
questions.stem: PJ Paint & Body sells car parts and provides services. PJ is contracted to provide ten replacement bumpers to Smyth New & Used Auto Mall. When should PJ recognize revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When all the bumpers are delivered to Smyth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: As soon as the purchase p rice is collected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: As each bumper is delivered to Smyth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When the contract is signed.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-19, each bumper is a distinct good; control transfers when each unit is delivered. Because each bumper can be used independently and is separately identifiable, there are ten separate performance obligations. Revenue is recognized as each bumper is delivered, not when the entire order is complete or when the contract is signed.

questions.subtopic: Cost-to-Cost Method вҖ” Multi-Year Profit Recognition
questions.stem:
ABC Co. is working on a construction contract. They have been recognizing costs and revenues from the contract as the performance obligation is being satisfied. The contract was started in Year 5 and was completed in Year 7 for a price of $9,000,000. The following cost information was provided:

| | December 31, Year 5 | December 31, Year 6 |
|---|---|---|
| Cumulative contract costs incurred | $3,900,000 | $6,000,000 |
| Estimated total cost at completion | $7,800,000 | $8,000,000 |

If ABC measures progress on the contract based on costs incurred, how much profit should the company recognize for the year ended December 31, Year 6?
Which of the following is a part of the core principle of the revenue recognition standard?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amount of revenue recognized represents the consideration the entity expects to receive in exchange for goods or services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If the expected cost of satisfying a performance obligation is greater than the amount of revenue allocated to it, the loss will be recognized when the cost is incurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recoverable incremental costs of obtaining a contract are capitalized and reported on the income statement in the same period in which the related revenue is recognized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To recognize revenues while the performance obligation is being satisfied, there must be some means for measuring progress toward completion of the obligation.

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-10-2 states the core principle: an entity recognizes revenue to depict the transfer of promised goods or services in an amount that reflects the consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for those goods or services. The other choices describe supplementary rules (onerous contract treatment, cost capitalization, and progress measurement) that are not part of the core principle itself.

questions.subtopic: Revenue Recognition at Point of Delivery
questions.stem: On October 1, Year 1, Acme Fuel Co. sold 100,000 gallons of heating oil to Karn Co. at $3 per gallon. Fifty thousand gallons were delivered on December 15, Year 1, and the remaining 50,000 gallons were delivered on January 15, Year 2. Payment terms were: 50% due on October 1, Year 1, 25% due on first delivery, and the remaining 25% due on second delivery. What amount of revenue should Acme recognize from this sale during Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-30, revenue is recognized when control of the goods transfers to the customer, not when payment is received. Only 50,000 gallons were delivered in Year 1 (December 15). Revenue = 50,000 Г— $3 = $150,000. The October 1 cash receipt of 50% ($150,000) is deferred as a contract liability until delivery occurs; the Year 2 delivery will trigger the remaining $150,000 of revenue.

questions.subtopic: Right of Return вҖ” Journal Entry Components
questions.stem: Baxley Co. makes a sale on account to a longtime customer on December 31, Year 5, for $3,000. The customer has 90 days to return the product if they are not satisfied with it. Based on prior experience, Baxley has determined that approximately 15% of similar products are returned within the acceptable 90-day period. The journal entry to record the sale on December 31, Year 5, should include which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A $3,000 debit to accounts receivable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A $450 debit to right to recover goods.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A $3,000 credit to sales revenue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A $2,550 credit to refund liability.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-23, when a right of return exists, the seller records: (1) revenue for the expected non-returned portion ($3,000 Г— 85% = $2,550 credit to sales revenue); (2) a refund liability for the expected returns ($3,000 Г— 15% = $450 credit); and (3) a right-to-recover-goods asset at the cost of the goods expected to be returned (debit). A $450 debit to right to recover goods is a correct element of the journal entry, representing the expected cost of goods to be returned. The accounts receivable debit should be $3,000 (the full amount owed), but the revenue credit is only $2,550, not $3,000.

questions.subtopic: Right of Return вҖ” Revenue Deferral When Returns Cannot Be Estimated
questions.stem: On May 1, Clothes Co. sells clothing to Link Corp. for $40,000. Clothes ships the clothing on May 1 and Link is obligated to pay Clothes within six months. Link is given 12 months to return any of the clothing for a refund if they experience low demand. Link is also given 18 months to exchange any clothing due to low demand. At the time of sale, Clothes cannot reasonably estimate returns, but estimates $5,000 in exchanged goods. Clothes should recognize revenue for the aforementioned transaction

choices.label: A
choices.text: On the day of the sale.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 months after the date of sale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 12 months after the date of sale.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 18 months after the date of sale.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-22, if an entity cannot reasonably estimate returns, it cannot recognize revenue until the return period expires or estimation becomes possible. Since Clothes cannot estimate refund returns, revenue must be deferred until the 12-month return (refund) period expires. The 18-month exchange period does not preclude revenue recognition because exchanges for equal or greater value do not reduce revenue; they are already accounted for through the $5,000 estimate.

questions.subtopic: Over-Time vs. Point-in-Time Revenue Recognition Criteria
questions.stem: An entity has agreed to a three-year contract with a customer. Recognizing revenue from long-term contracts over a period of time is not required when

choices.label: A
choices.text: The customer consumes the performance obligation as it is being delivered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The customer has direct use of the performance obligation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The customer has control over the performance obligation while it is being created.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The entity has no alternative use for the performance obligation.

questions.explanation: ASC 606-10-25-27 specifies three criteria for over-time recognition, any one of which is sufficient: (a) the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits, (b) the entity creates or enhances an asset that the customer controls as it is created, or (c) the entity's performance does not create an asset with alternative use to the entity AND the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. Having no alternative use for the asset alone (without the accompanying right to payment) does not by itself trigger over-time recognition.

questions.subtopic: Cost-to-Cost Method вҖ” Profit Calculation Inputs
questions.stem: Red Co. has a long-term construction contract that qualifies for recognition of revenue while the performance obligations are being met. Red will use costs incurred as the measure of progress toward completion of the project. In determining the amount of profit to recognize for the second year, which of the following items would Red not use in this calculation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Previously recognized profit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total estimated profit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progress bi llings to date.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total estimated cost.

questions.explanation: Under the cost-to-cost method per ASC 606-10-25-23, Year 2 profit = (cumulative costs incurred / total estimated costs) Г— total estimated profit вҲ’ previously recognized profit. The calculation requires: total estimated profit, total estimated cost, and previously recognized profit. Progress billings are used only for balance sheet presentation (to determine contract asset or liability) and have no role in computing profit recognized in a given period.

questions.subtopic: Construction Contract Journal Entries вҖ” Billings
questions.stem: Green Co. has two long-term construction contracts. One contract, with Blue Co., qualifies for revenue recognition while the performance obligation is being satisfied. The other contract, with Red Co., does not qualify for revenue recognition until the performance obligation is fully satisfied. For either of these two contracts, what account would be debited when preparing the journal entry to record billings? Blue Co. Red Co.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Billings Cash

choices.label: B
choices.text: Construction receivable Construction receivable

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cash Billings

choices.label: D
choices.text: Construction in progress Construction in progress

questions.explanation: When a contractor sends a billing (invoice) to a customer, the entry is: debit Accounts Receivable (or Construction Receivable) and credit the appropriate revenue or billings account. This applies to both over-time contracts (Blue Co.) and point-in-time contracts (Red Co.). Under the over-time method, the credit is to revenue; under the point-in-time method, the credit is to Billings on Construction in Progress. In both cases the debit is to Construction Receivable.

questions.stem: An auto repair company has agreed to provide a customer with a package of standard car maintenance services for $100. The package consists of an oil change, for which the company normally charges $75, and a tire rotation, for which the company normally charges $50. What amount of revenue should be recognized from the tire rotation?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-31, the transaction price is allocated to each distinct performance obligation based on relative standalone selling prices. Total standalone selling prices = $75 (oil change) + $50 (tire rotation) = $125. The tire rotation's share = $50 / $125 = 40%. Revenue allocated to tire rotation = 40% Г— $100 = $40.

questions.subtopic: Onerous Contract вҖ” Immediate Loss Recognition
questions.stem: On March 1, Bunyan Lumber entered into a contract to provide 20,000 feet of Brazilian cherry to Happy Homes, LLC, for $15 per foot, as requested from July to October. At the time, Bunyan could purchase Brazilian cherry for $10 per foot. The following events occurred after the contract was signed: In April, foresters identified a new disease affecting Brazilian cherry. In May, regulations were implemented that require transport inspections of Brazilian cherry. Due to the inspections and decreased supply, prices increased and fluctuated between $13 and $21 per foot. In June, Bunyan purchased 20,000 feet of Brazilian cherry for $16 per foot. Bunyan delivered 50% of the lumber in August and 50% in September. When should Bunyan recognize a loss related to this contract?

choices.label: A
choices.text: May.

choices.label: B
choices.text: June.

choices.label: C
choices.text: August.

choices.label: D
choices.text: August and September.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-7, when a contract becomes onerous вҖ” meaning total expected costs exceed total contract revenues вҖ” the expected loss must be recognized immediately. In May, prices fluctuated between $13 and $21; because the price could still be below the $15 contract price, a definitive loss was not yet determinable. In June, Bunyan actually purchased at $16 per foot, which is $1 above the $15 contract price, making the loss of $20,000 (20,000 Г— $1) certain and measurable. The full loss should be recognized in June.

questions.subtopic: Relative Standalone Selling Price Allocation вҖ” Multi-Component Bundle
questions.stem: Mod Cons Appliances entered into a contract to provide Reilly Clean Laundromat with 10 premium washer-dryer packages at $2,150 per package. The premium washer-dryer package includes a washing machine, a dryer, and a 5-year repair plan for each appliance. Mod Cons offers a washer package with a washer and a washer repair plan for $1,350 and a dryer package with a dryer and a dryer repair plan for $950. Mod Cons regularly sells individual washing machines at $1,200 each and individual dryers at $900 each. It sells 5-year repair plans at $300 for washing machines and $100 for dryers. Rounding to the nearest $100, how much of the Reilly contract revenue should be allocated to washing machines?
questions.explanation: Because the contract bundles two smaller bundles (a washer package and a dryer package) that each carry their own embedded discount, ASC 606-10-32-31 requires a step allocation: first allocate each smaller bundle's discount among its own components, then allocate any residual discount across all four performance obligations based on their discount-adjusted standalone prices. Washer package: standalone total $1,200 + $300 = $1,500 vs. bundle price $1,350, a $150 discount allocated 80%/20%, giving adjusted standalone prices of washer $1,080 and washer repair plan $270. Dryer package: standalone total $900 + $100 = $1,000 vs. bundle price $950, a $50 discount allocated 90%/10%, giving adjusted standalone prices of dryer $855 and dryer repair plan $95. The four adjusted standalone prices sum to $2,300 versus the actual package price of $2,150, leaving a further $150 discount allocated proportionally: washer allocation = $2,150 Г— ($1,080 / $2,300) вүҲ $1,009.57 per package. For 10 packages: $1,009.57 Г— 10 вүҲ $10,095.65, which rounds to the nearest $100 as $10,100.

questions.subtopic: Transaction Price Allocation вҖ” Bundled Products and Services
questions.stem: Niwot Co. sells products and service plans both separately and bundled together. Willy Loman, a Niwot Co. salesman, sold a Widgetron in 2014 for $1,000, its normal price, and told the customer he'd 'throw in the 3- year service plan for free', which the company normally sells for an additional $150. The product shipped, the customer paid, and $1,000 in revenue was recognized in 2014. Is this correct? Why or why not?

choices.label: A
choices.text: This is correct because the understanding between the seller and customer was that the product price was $1,000 and the service plan was free, which is in accordance with the principle of faithful representation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: This is incorrect because services must be rendered before any revenue can be recognized.

choices.label: C
choices.text: This is correct because the product shipped and the customer paid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: This is incorrect because the services are not truly free and service revenue must be allocated over the three years of the service plan.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-32-31, the transaction price of $1,000 must be allocated among all distinct performance obligations based on relative standalone selling prices. The product ($1,000 standalone) and the 3-year service plan ($150 standalone) are both distinct obligations. Allocated revenue: product = $1,000/$1,150 Г— $1,000 вүҲ $870; service = $150/$1,150 Г— $1,000 вүҲ $130, recognized ratably over three years. Recognizing the full $1,000 in 2014 overstates product revenue and omits the service revenue deferral required under ASC 606.

questions.subtopic: Anticipated Loss Recognition on Long-Term Contracts
questions.stem: When should an anticipated loss from a long-term contract be recognized if revenue from the contract is recognized over a period of time and at a point in time, respectively? Over a period of time At a point in time

choices.label: A
choices.text: Over the life of the project When the project is complete

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately When the project is complete

choices.label: C
choices.text: Over the life of the project Immediately

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediately Immediately

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-7, regardless of whether the performance obligation is satisfied over time or at a point in time, an anticipated loss on a contract must be recognized in full immediately when it becomes probable. This conservatism principle prevents deferring expected losses. There is no exception based on the revenue recognition method; both over-time and point-in-time contracts must recognize the full loss when the loss becomes foreseeable.

questions.subtopic: Contract Modifications вҖ” New Contract vs. Modification of Existing Contract
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, a furniture wholesale company agreed to sell 10 identical couches to a customer at a price of $1,000 per couch. The entity transfers control of each couch at the point in time it is delivered to the customer. By July 1, Year 1, 6 couches had been provided to the customer. On that date, the customer offered to purchase 2 additional couches of the same model at a price of $950 per couch. If the company agrees to accommodate the customer's request, does the modification in the order constitute a new contract?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the amount of consideration offered is not a material change from the price in the original contract.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the modification includes additional couches that are identical to those in the original contract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the modification was requested within one year of the original contract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the modification includes additional couches that are distinct from those in the original contract.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-12, a modification is treated as a new separate contract only if (1) the additional goods or services are distinct AND (2) the price of the additional goods reflects their standalone selling price. The additional couches are identical to the originals (distinct), but the offered price of $950 is below the $1,000 standalone selling price. Because condition (2) is not met, the modification is not a new contract; it is accounted for as a modification of the existing contract (prospectively, given the remaining couches are distinct). The most factually supported 'No' answer is that the couches are identical to those already in the contract, not a separate transaction at standalone pricing.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 606-10-25-12гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠгҖҒеҘ‘зҙ„еӨүжӣҙгҒҢж–°гҒҹгҒӘзӢ¬з«ӢеҘ‘зҙ„гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒв‘ иҝҪеҠ иІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒҢеҲҘеҖӢгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҖҒв‘ЎиҝҪеҠ иІЎгғ»гӮөгғјгғ“гӮ№гҒ®дҫЎж јгҒҢзӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж јгӮ’еҸҚжҳ гҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҖҒгҒ®дёЎж–№гӮ’жәҖгҒҹгҒҷеҝ…иҰҒгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮиҝҪеҠ гӮҪгғ•гӮЎгҒҜе…ғгҒЁеҗҢдёҖпјҲеҲҘеҖӢпјүгҒ гҒҢгҖҒжҸҗзӨәдҫЎж ј$950гҒҜзӢ¬з«ӢиІ©еЈІдҫЎж ј$1,000гӮ’дёӢеӣһгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒжқЎд»¶в‘ЎгӮ’жәҖгҒҹгҒ•гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮгҒ—гҒҹгҒҢгҒЈгҒҰж–°еҘ‘зҙ„гҒЁгҒҜгҒӘгӮүгҒҡгҖҒж—ўеӯҳеҘ‘зҙ„гҒ®еӨүжӣҙгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҮҰзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢпјҲж®ӢдҪҷгӮҪгғ•гӮЎгҒҢеҲҘеҖӢгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҒӢгӮүе°ҶжқҘзҡ„гҒ«еҮҰзҗҶпјүгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Revenue Recognition вҖ” Collectability and Consignment-Like Arrangements
questions.stem: On December 30, Devlin Co. sold goods to Jensen Co. for $10,000, under an arrangement in which (1) Jensen has an unlimited right of return and (2) Jensen's obligation to pay Devlin is contingent upon Jensen's reselling the goods. Past experience has shown that Jensen ordinarily resells 60% of goods and returns the other 40%. What amount should Devlin include in sales revenue for this transaction on its December 31 income statement?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-1, one of the criteria for revenue recognition is that collection of the consideration is probable. Here, Jensen's obligation to pay is contingent upon resale, meaning collection is not unconditional (or probable) at the time of sale. When the buyer's payment obligation is contingent on reselling to a third party, the arrangement resembles a consignment; revenue is recognized only when Jensen resells the goods. Devlin should recognize $0 on December 31.

questions.subtopic: Royalty Revenue вҖ” Net Sales Calculation
questions.stem: During 20X2, Fleet Co.'s trademark was licensed to Hitch Corp. for royalties of 10% of net sales of the trademarked items. Returns were estimated to be 1% of gross sales. On signing the licensing agreement, Hitch paid Fleet $75,000 as an advance against future royalty earnings. Gross sales of the trademarked items during the year were $600,000. What amount should Fleet report as royalty income for 20X2?
questions.explanation: Royalties are based on net sales. Net sales = $600,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 1%) = $594,000. Royalty income = 10% Г— $594,000 = $59,400. The $75,000 advance is treated as a prepayment (contract liability) and offset against earned royalties; it does not represent additional income beyond the $59,400 earned. Fleet reports $59,400 in royalty income for 20X2 (ASC 606-10-55-65).

questions.stem: A company produces three products. Normal pricing information on the products is as follows: Product A $80 Product B 20 Product C 60 The company regularly offers a discounted price of $90 when Products A and B are purchased together. If the company contracts to sell a package including all three products for a discounted price of $100, what amount of the discount should be allocated to Product A?
questions.explanation: Total standalone selling prices = $80 + $20 + $60 = $160; total contract discount = $160 вҲ’ $100 = $60. Because the company regularly sells A and B together for $90 (a $10 discount from their combined $100 standalone price), ASC 606-10-32-37 requires that observable $10 discount to be allocated only to A and B, based on their relative standalone selling prices: A = $80/$100 Г— $10 = $8; B = $20/$100 Г— $10 = $2. This leaves adjusted standalone prices of A = $72, B = $18, and C = $60 (total $150 = $90 bundle + $60 for C). The remaining discount of $50 ($150 вҲ’ $100) is then allocated across all three products based on these adjusted standalone prices: A = $72/$150 Г— $50 = $24; B = $18/$150 Г— $50 = $6; C = $60/$150 Г— $50 = $20. Total discount allocated to Product A = $8 + $24 = $32.

questions.subtopic: Point-in-Time Revenue Recognition вҖ” Completed Contract Method
questions.stem: A company accounts for a long-term construction contract by recognizing income and expenses at a point in time. Revenue is recognized when: (1) Cash is collected and (2) Progress billings exceed recorded costs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: (1) Yes, (2) Yes

choices.label: B
choices.text: (1) Yes, (2) No

choices.label: C
choices.text: (1) No, (2) Yes

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-30, for point-in-time recognition on construction contracts, revenue is recognized when control transfers to the customer upon contract completion, not when cash is collected and not when progress billings exceed recorded costs. Cash collection and billing milestones are factors in the balance sheet presentation of contract assets and liabilities, but neither triggers revenue recognition under the completed-contract (point-in-time) approach.

questions.subtopic: Measure of Progress for Over-Time Revenue Recognition
questions.stem: Acorn Software provides subscribers with access to web-based software for 365 days for a single payment, regardless of how often the customer uses the software. Acorn developed the software, regularly updates it, and provides 24-hour customer service access for subscribers at no extra charge. If the company recognizes revenue while the performance obligation is being satisfied, what base is most appropriate to measure progress toward completion of the obligation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Customer's monthly usage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Labor hours expended.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subscription time elapsed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subscriptions sold annually.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-31 and 606-10-25-33, the measure of progress should faithfully depict the entity's performance. The subscription grants continuous access to the software for 365 days regardless of usage; accordingly, the performance obligation is a stand-ready obligation satisfied evenly over time. Subscription time elapsed (straight-line over 365 days) best reflects this pattern. Customer usage is not relevant because the contract price does not vary with usage.

questions.subtopic: IP License Classification вҖ” Right to Access vs. Right to Use
questions.stem: Paw Gallery and Shell Portraits both offer downloadable photographs to their customers. Paw Gallery has a collection of animal photographs that may be downloaded by its subscribers. Subscribers pay an annual fee for an a ccess code allowing the user to download images from the site. Shell Portraits takes photographs at the customer's location. Shell emails each customer a unique code to access their photos once they are completed. Each access code is active for one week after it has been delivered. Both Paw and Shell allow unlimited reproduction of their images. When should Paw and Shell recognize revenue for these image sales? Paw Gallery Shell Portraits

choices.label: A
choices.text: At access code delivery At access code delivery

choices.label: B
choices.text: At access code delivery Over access duration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Over access duration At access code delivery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Over access duration Over access duration

questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-58 through 55-60, intellectual property licenses are classified as either a 'right to access' (over time) or a 'right to use' (point in time). Paw Gallery provides ongoing access to a changing collection (it likely updates the catalog), constituting a right to access вҖ” revenue over the subscription duration. Shell Portraits licenses specific photographs that are complete and unchanging; the code is merely the delivery mechanism for a one-time transfer of a specific asset, constituting a right to use вҖ” revenue at code delivery.

questions.subtopic: Transaction Price Allocation вҖ” Material Rights and Auto-Renewal Options
questions.stem: A company sells scented beauty products and personal care items. It is currently offering a promotion: customers can purchase a deluxe beauty kit for $500, which is its normal retail price, and get one month's free access to an online beauty consultant, which normally costs $40. To get the free access, customers must agree to a default auto - renewal of the service, cancellable at any time. The company estimates that the auto - renewal option will result in customers, on average, opting for six months of paid access after the free month expires. For each deluxe beauty kit sold as part of this promotion, how m uch revenue should be allocated to each performance obligation and how much deferred revenue should the company recognize? Beauty kit Beauty consultant Deferred revenue
questions.explanation: The transaction price is $500. The performance obligations are the beauty kit ($500 standalone) and one month of consultant access ($40 standalone). The auto-renewal option at market price is not a material right (customers pay the standard rate for subsequent months), so it does not create a separate performance obligation or deferred revenue. Total standalone prices = $500 + $40 = $540. Beauty kit allocation = $500/$540 Г— $500 вүҲ $462.96 вүҲ $463; consultant allocation = $40/$540 Г— $500 вүҲ $37.04 вүҲ $37. Deferred revenue = $0 because no material right exists.

questions.subtopic: Cost-to-Cost Method вҖ” Percentage of Completion Ratio
questions.stem: The calculation of income in Year 3 of a five - year construction contract accounted for using the cost - to - cost method includes the ratio of

choices.label: A
choices.text: Costs incurred in Year 3 to total billings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Costs incurred in Year 3 to total estimated costs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Total costs incurred to date to total billings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total costs incurred to date to total estimated costs.

questions.explanation: Under the cost-to-cost method per ASC 606-10-25-31, the percentage of completion is computed as cumulative costs incurred to date divided by total estimated costs (cumulative costs incurred to date plus estimated costs to complete). This cumulative-to-date ratio is then applied to total expected profit to determine cumulative income earned, from which previously recognized income is subtracted to get the current year amount. Billings do not appear in this ratio.

questions.subtopic: Net Sales вҖ” Returns vs. Exchanges
questions.stem: Fenn Stores, Inc. had sales of $1,000,000 during December, Year 2. Experience has shown that merchandise equaling 7% of sales will be returned within 30 days and that an additional 3% will be returned within 90 days. Returned merchandise is readily resalable . In addition, merchandise equaling 15% of sales will be exchanged for merchandise of equal or greater value. What amount should Fenn report for net sales in its income statement for the month of December, Year 2?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-55-22 and 55-23, revenue is reduced for expected returns (refund of consideration). Expected returns = 7% + 3% = 10% of $1,000,000 = $100,000. Net sales = $1,000,000 вҲ’ $100,000 = $900,000. Exchanges for equal or greater value are not returns; they do not reduce revenue because the entity retains equivalent consideration. The 15% exchange rate has no impact on net sales.

questions.subtopic: Revenue Recognition вҖ” Collectability and Nonrefundable Consideration
questions.stem: Bareb Co. sells 20 televisions to a new customer for $20,000. The customer is opening a sports bar, and Bareb is aware the sports bar will be subject to intense competition. Upon delivery, a $5,000 nonrefundable down payment is collected a nd the remaining $15,000 is due evenly over the next two years. If the customer defaults, Bareb's only recourse is to repossess the televisions. Bareb is uncertain as to the collectability of the remaining payments. What amount of revenue, if any, shoul d Bareb recognize upon transfer of the televisions?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 606-10-25-7(e), collection of the consideration must be probable for revenue recognition. Since Bareb is uncertain about the $15,000 balance and has only repossession rights, the $15,000 does not meet the collectability threshold. However, per ASC 606-10-25-7 and 606-10-25-9, when collectability is not probable, an entity recognizes revenue only to the extent it has received nonrefundable consideration and has no remaining obligation to transfer goods. The televisions have been delivered and the $5,000 is nonrefundable, so $5,000 of revenue is recognized upon delivery.

questions.subtopic: Right of Return вҖ” Operating Profit Calculation
questions.stem: Lew Co. sold 200,000 corrugated boxes for $2 each. Lew's cost was $1 per unit. The sales agreement gave the customer the right to return up to 60% of the boxes withi n the first six months, provided an appropriate reason was given. It was immediately determined, with appropriate reason, that 5% of the boxes would be returned. Lew absorbed an additional $10,000 to process the returns and expects to resell the boxes. Wh at amount should Lew report as operating profit from this transaction?
questions.explanation: Expected returns = 5% Г— 200,000 = 10,000 boxes. Net units sold = 190,000. Net revenue = 190,000 Г— $2 = $380,000. Cost of goods sold for 190,000 units = 190,000 Г— $1 = $190,000. Right to recover returned goods (asset) = 10,000 Г— $1 = $10,000 (offsets refund liability at cost). Return processing cost = $10,000 (expensed). Operating profit = $380,000 revenue вҲ’ $190,000 COGS вҲ’ $10,000 processing = $180,000. The right-to-recover asset and refund liability net to zero impact on profit.

questions.subtopic: Point-in-Time Recognition вҖ” Anticipated Loss on Onerous Contract
questions.stem:
The following data pertains to a company's long-term contracts, which commenced during Year 2:

| | Project 1 | Project 2 |
|---|---:|---:|
| Contract price | $420,000 | $300,000 |
| Costs incurred during Year 2 | $240,000 | $280,000 |
| Estimated costs to complete | $120,000 | $40,000 |
| Billed to customers during Year 2 | $150,000 | $270,000 |
| Received from customers during Year 2 | $90,000 | $250,000 |

If the company recognizes revenues and costs from contracts at a point in time, what amount of gross profit (loss) would the company report in its Year 2 income statement?
questions.explanation: Under point-in-time recognition, revenue on Project 1 and Project 2 is deferred until completion вҖ” neither was completed in Year 2, so no revenue or cost is recognized for either project. However, when a contract becomes a loss contract, the loss must be recognized immediately per ASC 606-10-25-7. Project 2 total estimated cost = $280,000 + $40,000 = $320,000 vs. contract price $300,000 вҶ’ anticipated loss = $20,000. Project 1 shows a profit ($420,000 price vs. $360,000 total cost), so no loss is recognized. Year 2 gross profit (loss) = $(20,000).
questions.explanationJa: зү№е®ҡжҷӮзӮ№иӘҚиӯҳгҒ§гҒҜгҖҒProject 1гғ»Project 2гҒЁгӮӮгҒ«е®ҢжҲҗгҒҫгҒ§еҸҺзӣҠиӘҚиӯҳгӮ’з№°гӮҠе»¶гҒ№гӮӢгҖӮз¬¬2е№ҙеәҰгҒ«гҒҜдёЎгғ—гғӯгӮёгӮ§гӮҜгғҲгҒЁгӮӮе®ҢжҲҗгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒеҸҺзӣҠгғ»иІ»з”ЁгҒҜиӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮгҒҹгҒ гҒ—гҖҒASC 606-10-25-7гҒ«гӮҲгӮҠжҗҚеӨұеҘ‘зҙ„гҒҜеҚіжҷӮиӘҚиӯҳгҒҢеҝ…иҰҒгҖӮProject 2гҒ®з·ҸиҰӢз©ҚеҺҹдҫЎпјқ$280,000пјӢ$40,000пјқ$320,000 vs. еҘ‘зҙ„йҮ‘йЎҚ$300,000 вҶ’ жҗҚеӨұ$20,000гҖӮProject 1гҒҜеҲ©зӣҠеҘ‘зҙ„пјҲ$420,000 vs. $360,000пјүгҒӘгҒ®гҒ§жҗҚеӨұгҒӘгҒ—гҖӮз¬¬2е№ҙеәҰгҒ®зІ—еҲ©зӣҠпјҲжҗҚеӨұпјүпјқ$(20,000)гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Interim Reporting вҖ” Property Taxes and Unanticipated Repairs
questions.stem: On January 16, Tree Co. paid $60,000 in property taxes on its factory for the current calendar year. On April 2, Tree paid $240,000 for unanticipated major repairs to its factory equipment. The repairs will benefit operations for the remainder of the calendar year. What amount of these expenses should Tree include in its third quarter interim financial statements for the three months ended September 30?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 270-10-45-10, costs and expenses that clearly benefit more than one interim period are allocated to those periods based on estimated time expired, benefit received, or activity associated with the periods, rather than expensed entirely when paid. Property taxes benefit the full year and are accrued ratably: Q3 share = 3/12 Г— $60,000 = $15,000. The major repairs, paid April 2, explicitly benefit operations for the remainder of the calendar year (April through December, 9 months), so the cost is allocated over those 9 months: Q3 share = 3/9 Г— $240,000 = $80,000. Total interim expense for the three months ended September 30 = $15,000 + $80,000 = $95,000.

questions.subtopic: Discontinued Operations вҖ” Income Statement Presentation
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Munn agreed to sell the assets and product line of one of its operating divisions for $1,600,000, which was expected to result in a gain on disposition. The sale was consummated on December 31, Year 2, and resulted in a gain on disposition of $450,000. This division's net operating losses were $250,000 in Year 1 and $320,000 in Year 2. The income tax rate for both years was 30%. In preparing its income statements, Munn should report which of the following amounts of gain (loss) from discontinued operations? Year 1 Year 2
questions.explanation: Under ASC 230-10 and ASC 205-20, discontinued operations include operating results (net of tax) each year and gain/loss on disposal in the year of actual sale. Year 1: operating loss = $250,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 30%) = $175,000 after-tax loss = $(175,000). A gain was anticipated in Year 1 but is not recognized until realized; the expected gain does not offset the Year 1 loss. Year 2: operating loss $(320,000) net of tax = $(224,000); gain on disposal $450,000 net of tax = $315,000; net Year 2 discontinued operations = $315,000 вҲ’ $224,000 = $91,000.

questions.stem: In financial reporting of segment data, which of the following would be used to determine a segment's operating income?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain or loss on discontinued operations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: General corporate expenses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intersegment sales.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Income tax expense.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 280-10-50-22, a segment's operating profit or loss is computed as revenue (including intersegment revenue) minus segment-level expenses. Intersegment sales are included in the measure of segment revenue used to evaluate performance. In contrast, gains or losses on discontinued operations, general corporate expenses, and income tax expense are not allocated to individual segments and are excluded from segment operating income.

questions.stem: In financial reporting of segment data, which of the following must be considered to determine whether an industry segment is a reportable segment? (1) Sales to unaffiliated customers and (2) Intersegment sales?

choices.label: A
choices.text: (1) Yes, (2) Yes

choices.label: B
choices.text: (1) Yes, (2) No

choices.label: C
choices.text: (1) No, (2) Yes

questions.explanation: Under ASC 280-10-50-12, a segment is reportable if its revenue (including both external sales to unaffiliated customers and intersegment sales) is 10% or more of the combined revenues of all operating segments. Both types of revenue must be considered in applying the 10% revenue threshold test for identifying reportable segments.

questions.stem: On December 1, Year 1, Pinn Corp., a calendar-year corporation, paid $40,000 for insurance to be used in calendar Year 2. On July 3, Year 2, Pinn prepaid $430,000 for advertising services. The advertising services will be provided over the remainder of the calendar year. What amount of these expenses should Pinn include in its September 30, Year 2 interim financial statements?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 270-10-45-9, costs that benefit two or more interim periods should be allocated among those periods. The $40,000 insurance covers all of Year 2 (4 quarters), so Q3 (JulyвҖ“September) receives $40,000 Г· 4 = $10,000. The $430,000 advertising is paid July 3 and benefits the remainder of Year 2 (July through December = 6 months); Q3 covers 3 of those 6 months, so Q3 receives $430,000 Г— 3/6 = $215,000. Total Q3 expense = $10,000 + $215,000 = $225,000.

questions.stem: On December 31, Year 1, Greer Co. entered into an agreement to sell its Hart segment's assets. On that date, Greer estimated the gain from the disposition of the assets in Year 2 would be $700,000. Hart's actual operating losses were $200,000 in Year 1 and $300,000 in Year 2. The actual gain on disposition of Hart's assets in Year 2 was $650,000. Disregarding income taxes, what net gain (loss) should be reported for discontinued operations in Greer's Year 1 and Year 2 income statements? Year 1 Year 2
questions.explanation: Under ASC 230 (and ASC 205-20), a gain on disposal of a discontinued component is recognized only when realized, not when estimated. In Year 1, only the actual operating loss of $200,000 is reported as discontinued operations. An anticipated gain is not recognized prospectively. In Year 2, the actual operating loss of $300,000 is offset by the actual gain on disposal of $650,000, producing net income from discontinued operations of $650,000 вҲ’ $300,000 = $350,000.

questions.subtopic: Interim Reporting - LIFO Inventory Loss Recovery
questions.stem: A company with a calendar year end uses the LIFO method to value its inventory in the annual and interim financial statements. On January 1, Year 2, the company's inventory balance was reported at $80,000. Market prices decreased in the second quarter, causing a potential realized inventory loss of $15,000. The decline was not expected to reverse by December 31, Year 2. However, the inventory's market price recovered, resulting in inventory valuations of $85,000 in the third quarter and $88,000 in the fourth quarter. At what amount should the company report inventory on its September 30, Year 2 interim balance sheet?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 270-10-45-6, when a LIFO inventory market decline in an interim period is not expected to reverse by year-end, the loss is recognized immediately, reducing inventory to $80,000 вҲ’ $15,000 = $65,000. When prices subsequently recover, ASC 270-10-45-7 permits recognition of the recovery up to the amount of the previously recognized loss. By Q3 the market value is $85,000, implying a full recovery of the $15,000 previously written down, so inventory is restored to $80,000 (original cost under LIFO; recovery cannot exceed the prior write-down).

questions.stem:
Cott Co.'s four business segments have revenues and identifiable assets expressed as percentages of Cott's total revenues and total assets as follows:

| | Revenues | Assets |
|---|---:|---:|
| Ebon | 64% | 66% |
| Fair | 14% | 18% |
| Gel | 14% | 4% |
| Hak | 8% | 12% |
| **Total** | **100%** | **100%** |

Which of these business segments are deemed to be reportable segments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ebon only.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ebon and Fair only.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ebon, Fair, and Gel only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ebon, Fair, Gel, and Hak.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 280-10-50-12, an operating segment is reportable if it meets any one of three 10% thresholds: (1) revenue вүҘ 10% of total revenues, (2) reported profit/loss вүҘ 10% of total absolute profit/loss, or (3) identifiable assets вүҘ 10% of total assets. Ebon (64% revenue, 66% assets), Fair (14% revenue, 18% assets), and Gel (14% revenue) each meet the revenue threshold. Hak has 8% revenue but 12% identifiable assets, meeting the assets threshold. Therefore all four segments вҖ” Ebon, Fair, Gel, and Hak вҖ” are reportable.

questions.stem: During the first quarter of Year 3, Tech Co. had income before taxes of $200,000, and its effective income tax rate was 15%. Tech's Year 2 effective annual income tax rate was 30%, but Tech expects its Year 3 effective annual income tax rate to be 25%. In its first quarter interim income statement, what amount of income tax expense should Tech report?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 740-270-25, interim income tax expense is computed using the estimated annual effective tax rate for the current year. The prior year rate of 30% and the current quarter rate of 15% are irrelevant. Tech expects its Year 3 effective annual rate to be 25%, so Q1 income tax expense = $200,000 Г— 25% = $50,000.

questions.stem: Opto Co. is a publicly traded, consolidated enterprise reporting segment information. Which of the following items is a required enterprise-wide disclosure regarding external customers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fact that transactions with a particular external customer constitute more than 10% of the total enterprise revenues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The identity of any external customer providing 10% or more of a particular operating segment's revenue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The identity of any external customer considered to be "major" by management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Information on major customers is not required in segment reporting.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 280-10-50-42, an entity must disclose the fact that revenues from transactions with a single external customer amount to 10% or more of the entity's revenues. The entity must also disclose the amount of revenue from each such customer and the identity of the segment(s) reporting the revenue. However, the entity is not required to disclose the identity of the customer itself. The threshold is 10% of total enterprise revenues, not 10% of a segment's revenues.

questions.subtopic: Discontinued Operations - Impairment and Operating Loss
questions.stem: On November 1, Year 2, management of Jinx Co. committed to a plan to dispose of Slim Co., a major subsidiary. The sale is to occur sometime in late Year 3. The disposal meets the requirements for classification as discontinued operations. The carrying value of Slim was $8,000,000, and management estimated the fair value less costs to sell to be $6,500,000. Slim had a loss of $2,000,000 for Year 2 and estimates losses for Year 3 of $3,000,000. Ignoring the tax effect, how much should Jinx report as loss from discontinued operations in its income statement for Year 2?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 205-20-45, once classified as held for sale, the disposal group is measured at the lower of carrying value or fair value less costs to sell, and any impairment is recognized immediately. The carrying value of $8,000,000 exceeds fair value less costs to sell of $6,500,000, creating an impairment loss of $1,500,000 recognized in Year 2. In addition, Slim's actual operating loss of $2,000,000 during Year 2 is recognized. Total Year 2 discontinued operations loss = $2,000,000 + $1,500,000 = $3,500,000. Estimated Year 3 losses of $3,000,000 are not recognized in Year 2.

questions.stem: On March 15, Year 2, Krol Co. paid property taxes of $90,000 on its office building for the calendar Year 2. On April 1, Year 2, Krol paid $150,000 for unanticipated repairs to its office equipment. The repairs will benefit operations for the remainder of Year 2. What is the total amount of these expenses that Krol should include in its quarterly income statement for the three months ended June 30, Year 2?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 270-10-45-9, property taxes that benefit the full year are allocated ratably across the four quarters: $90,000 Г· 4 = $22,500 per quarter allocated to Q2. Unanticipated repairs are expensed as incurred per ASC 270-10-45-14 because they were not anticipated at the start of the year; however, since the $150,000 incurred April 1 benefits the remainder of Year 2 (Q2 through Q4 = 9 months), it is allocated over those 9 months: Q2 receives $150,000 Г— 3/9 = $50,000. Total Q2 expense = $22,500 + $50,000 = $72,500.

questions.subtopic: Discontinued Operations - Net of Tax Presentation
questions.stem: For the eight months ended August 31, Year 5, the carpet division of a flooring company, which is considered a major line of business, had an operating loss of $115,000 from operations. On September 1, Year 5, the board of directors voted to discontinue the division's operations. On December 31, Year 5, the division was sold for a pretax loss of $135,000. The division's operating loss for Year 5 was $240,000. The company's income tax rate is 30%. What amount of loss should the company report as discontinued operations in the December 31, Year 5, income statement?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 205-20-45-3, all components of discontinued operations вҖ” both the full-year operating loss and any gain or loss on disposal вҖ” are aggregated and reported net of tax. Total pre-tax loss for Year 5 = operating loss of $240,000 + loss on disposal of $135,000 = $375,000. After applying the 30% income tax rate: $375,000 Г— (1 вҲ’ 0.30) = $262,500. The pre-decision operating loss (January through August) is still part of the discontinued operation's results for the year and cannot be excluded from the discontinued operations line.

questions.subtopic: Segment Reporting - Segment Operating Profit Calculation
questions.stem:
Graf Corp. discloses supplemental industry segment information. The following information is available for Year 1:

| Segment | Sales | Traceable operating expenses |
|---|---:|---:|
| X | $1,000,000 | $600,000 |
| Y | $800,000 | $500,000 |
| Z | $600,000 | $350,000 |

Graf incurred additional Year 1 indirect operating expenses of $360,000 and general corporate expenses of $240,000. Appropriate common expenses are allocated to segments based on the ratio of a segment's sales to total sales. What is Segment Z's Year 1 operating profit?
questions.explanation: Total sales = $1,000,000 + $800,000 + $600,000 = $2,400,000. Segment Z represents $600,000 / $2,400,000 = 25% of total sales. Indirect operating expenses of $360,000 are common expenses allocated to segments; Segment Z's allocation = $360,000 Г— 25% = $90,000. General corporate expenses of $240,000 are not allocated to individual segments under ASC 280. Segment Z operating profit = $600,000 revenue вҲ’ $350,000 traceable expenses вҲ’ $90,000 allocated indirect expenses = $160,000.

questions.stem: ABC Co. is a public company that is required to file financial reports with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). ABC acquired a significant related business, Bauer Co., through the registration and issuance of additional shares of common stock to the former stockholders of Bauer. Which of the following forms should ABC file with the SEC as a result of the acquisition of Bauer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Form 8-K.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Form 10-K.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Form 10-Q.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Form S-1.

questions.explanation: Form 8-K is the current report used to disclose material corporate events to the SEC on a timely basis (generally within 4 business days). A significant acquisition of another business is a triggering event requiring an 8-K filing. Form 10-K is the annual report, Form 10-Q is the quarterly report, and Form S-1 is a registration statement for a company's first public offering вҖ” none of these is the appropriate form for reporting a material acquisition event.

questions.stem: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulations for the financial statement presentation and disclosure requirements of SEC filings can be found in

choices.label: A
choices.text: Regulation S-B.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Regulation S-K.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regulation S-T.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regulation S-X.

questions.explanation: Regulation S-X contains the SEC's rules governing the form and content of financial statements and related schedules included in SEC filings. Regulation S-K contains non-financial disclosure requirements (management's discussion and analysis, risk factors, etc.). Regulation S-T governs the electronic submission of documents to the SEC's EDGAR system. Regulation S-B (now superseded) applied to smaller reporting companies.

questions.subtopic: Interim Reporting - Cumulative Income Tax Calculation
questions.stem:
In preparing its second quarter interim financial statements, a calendar year company reported the following information:

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| First quarter taxable income | $100,000 |
| First quarter income tax expense | 6,000 |
| Second quarter taxable income | 40,000 |
| As of the second quarter, the company had an estimated effective annual income tax rate of | 20 |

%. What amount should the company report as income tax expense in its interim income statement for the second quarter?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 740-270-30, interim income tax expense is computed cumulatively. Year-to-date income through Q2 = $100,000 + $40,000 = $140,000. At the estimated annual effective rate of 20%, year-to-date tax = $140,000 Г— 20% = $28,000. Tax already recognized in Q1 = $6,000 (the amount actually recorded, not the recomputed amount). Q2 income tax expense = $28,000 вҲ’ $6,000 = $22,000.

questions.stem: A U.S. public company with a worldwide public float of $800 million at the end of the second quarter of the fiscal year is required to file its annual report with the U.S. SEC on

choices.label: A
choices.text: Form 10-Q within 40 days after the end of the reporting period.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Form 10-Q within 45 days after the end of the reporting period.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Form 10-K within 60 days after the end of the reporting period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Form 10-K within 75 days after the end of the reporting period.

questions.explanation: A company with a worldwide public float of $700 million or more is classified as a large accelerated filer by the SEC. Large accelerated filers must file their annual report on Form 10-K within 60 days after the end of the fiscal year. Accelerated filers (float between $75M and $700M) have 75 days, and non-accelerated filers have 90 days. The 40- and 45-day periods apply to quarterly reports on Form 10-Q, not the annual report.

questions.stem:
A company has the following items on its year-end trial balance:

| Item | Amount |
|---|---:|
| Net sales | $500,000 |
| Common stock | 100,000 |
| Insurance expense | 75,000 |
| Wages | 50,000 |
| Cost of goods sold | 100,000 |
| Cash | 40,000 |
| Accounts payable | 25,000 |
| Interest payable | 20,000 |

What is the company's gross profit?
questions.explanation: Gross profit is computed as net sales minus cost of goods sold. Gross profit = $500,000 вҲ’ $100,000 = $400,000. Operating expenses such as insurance expense and wages are deducted after gross profit to arrive at operating income. Balance sheet items (common stock, cash, accounts payable, interest payable) are not included in the income statement calculation.

questions.subtopic: Interim Reporting - LIFO Inventory Loss and Recovery
questions.stem: A company with a calendar year end uses the LIFO method to value inventory in its annual and interim financial statements. The company realizes a $10,000 loss due to declining inventory prices in the first quarter. The company expects the loss to be permanent. However, market prices increase by $3,000 in the second quarter and $5,000 in the third quarter. What amounts of loss and loss recovery, if any, should the company recognize in its interim financial statements for the first and third quarters? First quarter Third quarter

choices.label: C
choices.text: $10,000 loss $5,000 loss recovery

choices.label: D
choices.text: $10,000 loss $8,000 loss recovery

questions.explanation: Under ASC 270-10-45-6, a LIFO inventory loss expected to be permanent in an interim period is recognized immediately in that period. Therefore, the $10,000 loss is recognized in Q1. Under ASC 270-10-45-7, subsequent recoveries of previously recognized inventory write-downs may be recognized up to the amount of previously recognized losses. In Q2, $3,000 of recovery is recognized. In Q3, an additional $5,000 recovery is recognized, as total recovery ($3,000 + $5,000 = $8,000) does not exceed the original $10,000 loss. Q3 specifically recognizes the $5,000 recovery.

questions.subtopic: Financial Statement Disclosures - Significant Accounting Policies
questions.stem: The summary of significant accounting policies should disclose the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Effect of a retroactive application of an accounting change.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Basis of profit recognition on long-term contracts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adequacy of pension plan assets in relation to vested benefits.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Future minimum lease payments in the aggregate and for each of the five succeeding fiscal years.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 235-10-50-1, the summary of significant accounting policies should identify and describe the accounting principles used and methods of applying those principles when alternatives exist, including the revenue recognition policy and basis of profit recognition for long-term contracts. The effect of a retroactive accounting change is disclosed separately (not in the accounting policy note). Pension funding adequacy and future minimum lease payments are separate footnote disclosures, not accounting policy disclosures.

questions.subtopic: Financial Statement Disclosures - Significant Accounting Policies
questions.stem: Which of the following information should be included in Melay, Inc.'s Year 2 summary of significant accounting policies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Property, plant, and equipment depreciation is computed by the straight-line method.

choices.label: B
choices.text: During Year 2, the Delay segment was sold at a significant loss.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reportable segment Year 2 sales are Alay $1M, Belay $2M, and Celay $3M.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Future common share dividends are expected to approximate 60% of earnings.

questions.explanation: The summary of significant accounting policies should describe the specific accounting principles used and the methods of applying them, such as the depreciation method for property, plant, and equipment. Under ASC 235-10-50-3, this note is where entities disclose their accounting methodology choices. The sale of a segment is a specific transaction disclosed elsewhere in the notes. Segment sales figures belong in segment disclosures. Future dividend payout ratios are management projections, not accounting policies.
